This is **Tandoori Chicken (half chicken, marinated)**. Tandoori Chicken is a North Indian dish of whole chicken or chicken pieces marinated in yoghurt and a vibrant paste of tandoori masala, turmeric, red chilli, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice, then cooked in a tandoor clay oven at temperatures exceeding 400°C, imparting a characteristic charred, smoky exterior with juicy, spiced meat inside. The vivid orange-red colour comes from the combination of turmeric, red chilli, and sometimes food colouring. It is served with mint chutney, sliced raw onion, and lemon. It is one of the most popular Indian dishes in Singapore's Indian restaurants and is widely available at hawker stalls. Per 100g: 163.0 kcal, 24.2g protein, 6.8g fat, 2.5g carbohydrates, 0.2g dietary fibre, 382.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1146mg per half-chicken serving (300g) — about 50% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the marinade salt and tandoori masala are the primary sodium contributors. Patients with hypertension may consume Tandoori Chicken in moderate portions; requesting the marinade with reduced salt and consuming without additional sauces will keep sodium at a manageable level. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrate content (~2.5g/100g); extremely high protein content (24g/100g); negligible glycaemic impact from the chicken itself. Tandoori Chicken is an excellent high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for diabetic patients; pair with a salad and limit accompanying rice or naan to optimise blood glucose management. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. High protein from lean chicken supports muscle maintenance; turmeric, ginger, and cumin in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory curcumin, gingerol, and cuminaldehyde. Tandoori Chicken is an excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the lean protein and anti-inflammatory spice marinade make it one of the most beneficial Indian meat preparations for inflammatory conditions. Note: One of the lowest-calorie, highest-protein options in this dataset. Contains dairy (yoghurt). The skin-on preparation adds approximately 15–20% more fat. Skinless, boneless tandoori breast is significantly lower in fat (~4g/100g). The red colour from chilli and turmeric does not add measurable nutrients at marinating quantities.

This is **Tandoori Chicken (half chicken, marinated)**. This is **Samosa (potato and peas, fried)**. Samosa is a classic Indian fried snack of a triangular wheat flour pastry shell filled with a spiced mashed potato and pea mixture seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chilli, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. It is typically served with sweet tamarind chutney and fresh mint-coriander chutney. Samosas are vegetarian and vegan in the standard aloo (potato) preparation, and are widely available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and bakeries across Singapore as a popular snack at all times of day. Per 100g: 265.0 kcal, 5.8g protein, 14.2g fat, 30.5g carbohydrates, 2.8g dietary fibre, 318.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fried wheat flour pastry with potato filling; GI estimated from refined wheat and potato components. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 477mg per 2-piece serving (150g) — about 21% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; relatively low-sodium for a fried snack. Patients with hypertension may consume samosas occasionally; the tamarind and mint chutneys served alongside are additional sodium sources that should be used sparingly. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Refined wheat flour pastry has a high GI (~65); potato filling adds high GI starch; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 29 is significant for a snack-sized portion. Diabetic patients should limit samosa consumption to an occasional treat, preferring baked over deep-fried versions, and pairing with a protein-rich accompaniment to moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Deep-frying creates pro-inflammatory compounds (acrylamide, advanced glycation end products); refined wheat is pro-inflammatory at high consumption; however, cumin and coriander provide anti-inflammatory phytocompounds. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit fried samosa consumption; baked samosas are a preferable alternative, and the anti-inflammatory spices in the filling provide modest benefit. Note: The standard potato-pea samosa is vegetarian and vegan (no dairy in the dough in the standard recipe). Cholesterol is 0.0g as this is a vegan dish. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (no animal products). Meat samosas (chicken or lamb) will have significantly different nutritional profiles. Chutney accompaniments are not included in the per_100g values.

This is **Masala Dosa (with potato masala filling)**. Masala Dosa is a classic South Indian dish of a large, paper-thin, crisply golden fermented rice and urad dal crepe wrapped around a spiced potato masala filling of boiled potatoes seasoned with mustard seeds, turmeric, curry leaves, and green chilli. The batter is made by soaking and grinding rice and urad dal, then fermenting the mixture overnight to develop its characteristic slightly sour flavour and the crisp, porous texture when cooked on a hot griddle. It is served with coconut chutney and sambar (lentil vegetable soup). It is one of the most popular breakfast and light meal dishes at South Indian and Tamil restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 166.0 kcal, 4.6g protein, 6.8g fat, 22.8g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 278.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 66 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 42.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — dosa (fermented rice and lentil pancake); GI elevated by the starchy rice base despite fermentation. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 778mg per serving (280g) — about 34% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lower-sodium Indian hawker dishes. Patients with hypertension may consume Masala Dosa regularly; the coconut chutney and sambar served alongside are the main sodium sources and can be consumed in moderation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Fermented rice base has a high GI (~66) despite fermentation; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 42 is significant; potato filling adds further starchy carbohydrates. Diabetic patients should consume Masala Dosa in moderation and pair it with a high-fibre sambar; the fermentation process improves digestibility but does not substantially lower the GI of the rice-based dosa. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Completely vegan and dairy-free; turmeric and mustard seeds in the potato filling provide anti-inflammatory compounds; fermented dosa batter improves gut microbiome diversity. Masala Dosa is an excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; it is entirely plant-based with anti-inflammatory spices, and the fermented batter may support gut health which is linked to immune regulation. Note: Fully vegan and dairy-free in the standard preparation (no ghee used). Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0 as there are no animal products. Versions with ghee (butter) on the dosa will have higher saturated fat. The sambar accompaniment adds approximately 30–40 kcal and significant potassium, folate, and fibre when consumed.

The batter is made by soaking and grinding rice and urad dal, then fermenting the mixture overnight to develop its characteristic slightly sour flavour and the crisp, porous texture when cooked on a hot griddle. It is served with coconut chutney and sambar (lentil vegetable soup). It is one of the most popular breakfast and light meal dishes at South Indian and Tamil restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 166.0 kcal, 4.6g protein, 6.8g fat, 22.8g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 278.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 66 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 42.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — dosa (fermented rice and lentil pancake); GI elevated by the starchy rice base despite fermentation. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 778mg per serving (280g) — about 34% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lower-sodium Indian hawker dishes. Patients with hypertension may consume Masala Dosa regularly; the coconut chutney and sambar served alongside are the main sodium sources and can be consumed in moderation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Fermented rice base has a high GI (~66) despite fermentation; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 42 is significant; potato filling adds further starchy carbohydrates. Diabetic patients should consume Masala Dosa in moderation and pair it with a high-fibre sambar; the fermentation process improves digestibility but does not substantially lower the GI of the rice-based dosa. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Completely vegan and dairy-free; turmeric and mustard seeds in the potato filling provide anti-inflammatory compounds; fermented dosa batter improves gut microbiome diversity. Masala Dosa is an excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; it is entirely plant-based with anti-inflammatory spices, and the fermented batter may support gut health which is linked to immune regulation. Note: Fully vegan and dairy-free in the standard preparation (no ghee used). Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0 as there are no animal products. Versions with ghee (butter) on the dosa will have higher saturated fat. The sambar accompaniment adds approximately 30–40 kcal and significant potassium, folate, and fibre when consumed.

This is **Thosai / Dosa (plain, no filling)**. Plain Thosai (Dosa) is the foundational South Indian fermented rice and urad dal crepe, made from a batter of soaked rice and dehusked black lentils that is fermented overnight until naturally sour and lightly effervescent, then ladled onto a hot flat griddle and spread in circular motions to create a large, thin, lacy crepe. The plain version is golden-brown, crisp at the edges, and soft in the centre, served with coconut chutney and sambar. It is gluten-free, vegan, and one of the most widely consumed South Indian breakfast items at Singapore's Indian restaurants and mamak stalls. Per 100g: 183.0 kcal, 5.2g protein, 6.5g fat, 28.2g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 185.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 68 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 19.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — plain dosa (fermented rice pancake, griddled); GI higher than expected due to the gelatinised rice starch. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 185mg per thosai (100g) — about 8% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lowest-sodium carbohydrate staples in Singapore's hawker landscape. Patients with hypertension may consume plain thosai freely; the coconut chutney and sambar served alongside are the main sodium contributors and can be moderated. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Plain dosa has a relatively high GI (~68) for a fermented product; however, its per-piece glycaemic load (~19) is manageable; fermentation reduces the glycaemic impact compared to non-fermented rice. Diabetic patients can consume 1–2 plain thosai per meal, ideally with sambar and chutney which add protein and fibre; the small per-piece portion keeps the glycaemic load moderate. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Completely vegan and anti-inflammatory; fermented batter promotes gut microbiome health which supports immune regulation; urad dal provides folate and plant protein. Plain thosai is an excellent choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the fermented preparation, gluten-free composition, and vegan profile make it anti-inflammatory, and it pairs well with anti-inflammatory sambar for a complete meal. Note: Fully vegan. No animal products. GI is higher than expected for a fermented product because the rice starch gelatinisation during cooking largely offsets the acid-mediated GI reduction from fermentation. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. The coconut chutney and sambar accompaniments are not included in per_100g values.

This is **Dal Curry (yellow lentil, tarka dal)**. Dal Curry (Tarka Dal) is a foundational Indian dish of slow-cooked yellow lentils (typically toor dal or chana dal) simmered until soft in a mildly spiced broth with turmeric, tomatoes, and onions, then finished with a tarka — a hot oil or ghee tempering of cumin seeds, garlic, dried chillies, and curry leaves poured sizzling over the dal. It is served as a side dish alongside rice, roti, thosai, or prata, and is one of the most nutritionally dense, high-protein, high-fibre, and low-GI items in Singapore's Indian hawker repertoire. Per 100g: 105.0 kcal, 6.2g protein, 3.5g fat, 13.5g carbohydrates, 3.2g dietary fibre, 282.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 29 (Low (≤55)). This is **Cooked Yellow Noodle / Mee (plain, drained)**. Yellow egg noodles (commonly called 'mee' in Singapore) are thick, springy wheat and egg noodles with a characteristic yellow colour from the kansui (alkaline lye water) used in their production, which also gives them their distinctive bouncy texture. They are the noodle base for many Singapore hawker classics including Hokkien Mee, Mee Goreng, and Wonton Mee. The fresh noodles are pre-cooked (parboiled) by the manufacturer and need only brief blanching before use. Per 100g: 118.0 kcal, 3.8g protein, 0.6g fat, 24.5g carbohydrates, 0.9g dietary fibre, 86.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 25.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fresh yellow wheat noodles (boiled, drained); lower GI than dried noodles due to fresh egg noodle structure. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 172mg per serving (200g) of plain noodles — less than 8% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; sodium burden in noodle dishes comes primarily from sauces and seasonings. Patients with hypertension should manage sodium through the sauces and seasonings in noodle dishes rather than the noodles themselves; plain cooked yellow noodles are low in sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Medium GI (~52); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 26 is moderate; fresh egg noodles have a more favourable GI than dried rice noodles or white rice. Yellow egg noodles are a more diabetes-friendly carbohydrate base than white rice or dried rice noodles; diabetic patients can include a 150–200g cooked portion in meals paired with sufficient protein and fibre. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Plain wheat egg noodles are nutritionally neutral for inflammation; they provide moderate energy without pro-inflammatory fats or sugars. Plain yellow noodles are an acceptable staple for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the alkaline kansui adds no inflammatory burden, and the egg content provides modest selenium and B vitamins. Note: Values represent plain cooked drained noodles with no added sauces or seasonings. The kansui (alkaline water) in yellow noodles is the source of the distinctive yellow colour and springy texture but adds no significant nutrients. Sodium is minimal without added seasonings.

This is **Cooked Flat Rice Noodle / Kway Teow (plain)**. Flat rice noodles (kway teow) are wide, flat, silky white noodles made from rice flour and water, sold fresh in Singapore's hawker centres and wet markets. They have a soft, slippery texture and a mild, clean rice flavour that absorbs sauces and wok char exceptionally well, making them the preferred base for Char Kway Teow. Fresh kway teow are entirely allergen-free (no wheat, no egg), gluten-free, and vegan. Per 100g: 109.0 kcal, 1.8g protein, 0.2g fat, 25.8g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 5.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 61 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles, freshly made (cooked); GI moderate-high due to rice flour starch gelatinisation. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 10mg per serving (200g) of plain noodles — essentially sodium-free; all sodium in kway teow dishes comes from sauces and seasonings. Patients with hypertension should manage sodium through dish sauces rather than the kway teow noodles, which contain negligible sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Moderate GI (~61) from gelatinised rice flour; per-serving glycaemic load approximately 32; slightly higher GI than yellow egg noodles due to absence of egg. Diabetic patients should be aware that flat rice noodles have a moderately higher GI than yellow egg noodles or pasta; portion control to 150–200g cooked is advisable. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Nutritionally neutral for inflammation; gluten-free and allergen-free; provides clean energy without inflammatory compounds. Flat rice noodles are suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, particularly those following a gluten-free diet; they provide clean, easily digestible carbohydrate energy without pro-inflammatory additives. Note: Vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free. Nutritionally simple — primarily starch and water. The low protein content means kway teow must be paired with protein-rich toppings for nutritional completeness. Fresh kway teow has a shorter shelf life than dried rice noodles.

This is **Cooked Rice Vermicelli / Bee Hoon (plain)**. Rice vermicelli (bee hoon in Singapore; bihun in Malay/Indonesian) is the thin, dried rice flour noodle that forms the base for dishes like Laksa, Mee Siam, fried bee hoon, and noodle soups across Southeast Asia. Made entirely from rice flour and water, dried rice vermicelli is first soaked in hot water then briefly cooked to restore its soft, springy texture. It is gluten-free, vegan, and allergen-free — the most universally inclusive noodle staple in Singapore's diverse food scene. Per 100g: 109.0 kcal, 2.0g protein, 0.2g fat, 24.5g carbohydrates, 0.4g dietary fibre, 4.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 47 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 21.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli (dried, then cooked); lower GI than fresh rice noodles due to retrogradation of starch during drying and rehydration. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 7mg per serving (180g) — essentially sodium-free; all sodium in bee hoon dishes comes from sauces and seasonings. Patients with hypertension may freely include rice vermicelli as the noodle base; all sodium management should focus on the sauces and toppings served alongside. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Rice vermicelli has a relatively low GI (~47) for a rice-based product due to starch retrogradation during drying; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 21 is moderate-low. Rice vermicelli is a more diabetes-friendly noodle base than white rice or fresh rice noodles; its lower GI from starch retrogradation makes it a preferable carbohydrate choice in hawker meals. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Nutritionally neutral; gluten-free and allergen-free; provides clean carbohydrate energy without inflammatory burden. Rice vermicelli is suitable for all patients including those with rheumatoid arthritis; its gluten-free composition is beneficial for patients who also have gluten sensitivity, which is associated with some autoimmune conditions. Note: Nutritionally the leanest noodle option. Essentially pure starch and water with negligible protein, fat, and micronutrients. The GI benefit over white rice and fresh rice noodles comes from retrogradation of starch during the drying process. Must be paired with protein and fibre-rich accompaniments for nutritional completeness.

This is **Roti Canai (plain, without curry)**. Roti Canai (also called roti prata in Singapore) is a Malaysian Indian Muslim flatbread of refined wheat flour dough enriched with ghee or margarine, stretched by repeatedly folding and tossing the dough to develop multiple layers, then cooked on a greased flat griddle until golden and flaky. The plain (kosong) version is served with dhal curry and fish or chicken curry for dipping. It is the most popular breakfast item at Malaysian mamak stalls and is widely enjoyed in Singapore as well, where it is called prata. Per 100g: 302.0 kcal, 6.8g protein, 13.5g fat, 40.2g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 380.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 24.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white flour paratha (pan-fried); same GI category as Singapore prata. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 380mg per piece (100g) — about 17% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; two pieces commonly consumed would provide 34% of the daily limit from the roti alone. Patients with hypertension should limit roti canai to one piece and moderate the curry dipping sauce; the dough contains moderate salt but the curry accompaniment significantly increases sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Refined wheat flour has a high GI (~62); high fat content from ghee slows but does not prevent a significant postprandial glucose rise; per-piece glycaemic load is approximately 25. Diabetic patients should limit roti canai to one piece per meal and replace with whole wheat paratha where available; the high fat and refined carbohydrate combination produces a sustained but significant glucose rise. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Moderate saturated fat from ghee or margarine; refined wheat flour is pro-inflammatory at high consumption frequencies. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit roti canai to occasional consumption; choosing whole wheat versions and reducing ghee will lower the pro-inflammatory impact of regular consumption. Note: Roti Canai (Malaysia) and Roti Prata (Singapore) are the same dish with different regional names. Nutrient values are virtually identical. Trans fat content depends on whether partially hydrogenated margarine is used. Ghee versions have higher saturated fat; margarine versions may have trans fat. The curry accompaniment is not included in per_100g values.

This is **Egg Fried Rice (Chinese hawker style)**. Chinese Egg Fried Rice is a fundamental hawker dish of day-old jasmine rice stir-fried in a screaming-hot wok with beaten egg scrambled directly into the rice, seasoned with light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper, and finished with sliced spring onion. The use of cold day-old rice is essential for achieving the characteristic separate, slightly dry, wok-fragrant grains rather than a wet, mushy result. It is one of the most widely available and universally consumed dishes across all of Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops. Per 100g: 182.0 kcal, 5.6g protein, 6.8g fat, 25.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 478.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 68 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 55.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fried rice (cooked jasmine rice, stir-fried); GI similar to plain white rice but glycaemic load elevated by the larger serving size of fried rice compared to steamed rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1530mg per serving (320g) — about 66% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by soy sauce and sesame oil seasoning. Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in fried rice; using light soy sauce sparingly rather than dark soy sauce can meaningfully reduce sodium content. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Jasmine rice base has a high GI (~68); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 56 is high; fried rice dishes typically use larger rice volumes than steamed rice bowls. Diabetic patients should be aware that fried rice has a high glycaemic load comparable to plain white rice; limiting portion to 200g and increasing protein additions (extra egg, chicken) will improve the glycaemic outcome. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate fat with good unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio from cooking oil; eggs provide selenium and vitamin D; spring onion provides quercetin. Egg Fried Rice is an acceptable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the egg component provides valuable anti-inflammatory nutrients, and sesame oil contains lignans with potential anti-inflammatory benefits. Note: Vegetarian in the standard preparation (egg, soy, no meat). Can be made vegan by omitting egg. The high GI is nutritionally significant — fried rice provides essentially the same glycaemic impact as plain white rice. Yang Chow fried rice with char siu and shrimp will have higher protein and calorie content.

Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Jasmine rice base has a high GI (~68); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 56 is high; fried rice dishes typically use larger rice volumes than steamed rice bowls. Diabetic patients should be aware that fried rice has a high glycaemic load comparable to plain white rice; limiting portion to 200g and increasing protein additions (extra egg, chicken) will improve the glycaemic outcome. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate fat with good unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio from cooking oil; eggs provide selenium and vitamin D; spring onion provides quercetin. Egg Fried Rice is an acceptable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the egg component provides valuable anti-inflammatory nutrients, and sesame oil contains lignans with potential anti-inflammatory benefits. Note: Vegetarian in the standard preparation (egg, soy, no meat). Can be made vegan by omitting egg. The high GI is nutritionally significant — fried rice provides essentially the same glycaemic impact as plain white rice. Yang Chow fried rice with char siu and shrimp will have higher protein and calorie content.

This is **Char Siu (BBQ Roast Pork, Cantonese)**. Char Siu is a Cantonese Chinese BBQ pork preparation of pork neck or tenderloin marinated in a sweet-savoury glaze of hoisin sauce, honey, soy sauce, oyster sauce, five-spice powder, and sugar, then roasted in a traditional oven on long skewers (叉, 'fork') at high heat until caramelised and lacquered with a distinctive sticky, reddish-brown coating. It is a staple of siu mei (roast meats) shops across Singapore and Hong Kong, served sliced over rice with blanched vegetables and sweet gravy. It is also a key ingredient in Wonton Mee, BBQ pork buns, and many other Cantonese dishes. Per 100g: 310.0 kcal, 22.5g protein, 18.2g fat, 13.5g carbohydrates, 0.2g dietary fibre, 682.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1023mg per 150g serving — about 44% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit for the protein portion alone; combined with rice it forms a high-sodium meal. Patients with hypertension should limit the char siu portion to 100g and request less marinade sauce; the sweet marinade is high in both sodium and sugar. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. High sugar content from honey and hoisin marinade (~11g sugar/100g); the caramelised surface crust concentrates sugars; however, the protein and fat content modulates the glycaemic impact when served with rice. Diabetic patients should be aware of the high added sugar in the char siu marinade; request reduced sauce drizzle and limit portion to 100–120g to manage sugar intake. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Pork provides good B vitamins and zinc; however, the high processed sugar content and roasting at high temperature creates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) which are pro-inflammatory. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume char siu occasionally; the high-heat caramelisation creates AGEs that can promote inflammation, though the pork protein provides beneficial micronutrients including zinc and vitamin B12. Note: Not halal due to pork. Halal chicken char siu versions exist and have lower fat but similar sugar content. The distinctive red colour from red food colouring or red yeast rice adds no measurable nutritional value. Thiamine (B1) content is notably high from pork, making char siu a good source of vitamin B1.

This is **Stir-fried Kangkung with Belacan**. Stir-fried Kangkung with Belacan is one of Singapore's most popular vegetable side dishes — fresh water spinach (kangkung; Ipomoea aquatica) stir-fried in a blazing-hot wok with pounded toasted belacan (fermented shrimp paste), dried shrimp, garlic, and fresh or dried chilli until wilted with a slight char and an intensely savoury, umami-rich, spicy flavour. Kangkung is a semi-aquatic tropical leafy green with hollow stems and tender leaves, exceptional in vitamin A, folate, and vitamin C. It is a staple side dish at zi char (Chinese stir-fry) stalls and Malay hawker stalls throughout Singapore and Malaysia. Per 100g: 62.0 kcal, 3.1g protein, 4.0g fat, 3.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 512.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1024mg per serving (200g) — about 45% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by belacan (shrimp paste) and dried shrimp. This is **Silken Tofu (steamed, plain)**. Silken tofu is the softest variety of tofu — a delicate, smooth, custard-like soy protein product made from minimally processed whole soybean milk coagulated with gypsum (calcium sulfate), nigari, or GDL to form a smooth, fragile curd with very high water content. In Singapore, steamed silken tofu is typically dressed with a light sauce of soy, sesame oil, and spring onion or served in soups and stews. It is one of the most versatile, nutritious, and allergy-friendly protein foods available — high in protein, calcium, and soy isoflavones with negligible carbohydrate content. Per 100g: 55.0 kcal, 4.8g protein, 2.5g fat, 2.0g carbohydrates, 0.3g dietary fibre, 12.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 15 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 0.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — tofu (all types); GI is very low due to minimal carbohydrate content and the protein-fat composition. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 18mg per serving (150g) — less than 1% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; silken tofu is one of the lowest-sodium protein foods available. Patients with hypertension may consume silken tofu freely; the isoflavones in soy have been shown to have a modest blood pressure-lowering effect, and the low sodium makes it an ideal protein food for hypertensive patients. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Extremely low GI (~15) and negligible glycaemic load; high protein content moderates blood glucose responses; soy isoflavones may improve insulin sensitivity. Silken tofu is one of the most diabetes-friendly protein foods available; its extremely low GI, high protein, and low carbohydrate content make it an ideal component of a diabetic meal plan. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Soy isoflavones have demonstrated anti-inflammatory effects in rheumatoid arthritis research; high calcium and magnesium support bone health which is compromised in RA; completely plant-based. Silken tofu is an excellent food for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the soy isoflavones provide anti-inflammatory benefit, the calcium supports bone health, and it is a low-inflammatory-load protein alternative to meat. Note: Calcium content is notably high (111mg/100g) when coagulated with gypsum (calcium sulfate); tofu made with nigari (magnesium chloride) will have lower calcium and higher magnesium. Vegan and fully plant-based. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. The dressing (soy sauce, sesame oil) adds sodium and should be accounted for separately.

Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 18mg per serving (150g) — less than 1% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; silken tofu is one of the lowest-sodium protein foods available. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Silken tofu is an excellent food for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the soy isoflavones provide anti-inflammatory benefit, the calcium supports bone health, and it is a low-inflammatory-load protein alternative to meat. Note: Calcium content is notably high (111mg/100g) when coagulated with gypsum (calcium sulfate); tofu made with nigari (magnesium chloride) will have lower calcium and higher magnesium. This is **Silken Tofu (steamed, plain)**. Silken tofu is the softest variety of tofu — a delicate, smooth, custard-like soy protein product made from minimally processed whole soybean milk coagulated with gypsum (calcium sulfate), nigari, or GDL to form a smooth, fragile curd with very high water content. In Singapore, steamed silken tofu is typically dressed with a light sauce of soy, sesame oil, and spring onion or served in soups and stews. This is **Half-Boiled Eggs (Singapore style, 2 eggs)**. Singapore-style half-boiled eggs are a kopitiam (coffee shop) institution — two fresh eggs lowered into a closed container of just-boiled water for exactly 7–10 minutes, producing whites that are just barely set and opaque while the yolks remain completely liquid and rich. The eggs are broken into a small bowl and consumed immediately, seasoned with a few drops of dark soy sauce and a generous pinch of white pepper. They are always served as part of the Kaya Toast Set and are one of the simplest, most nutritionally complete foods in Singapore's hawker culture. Per 100g: 152.0 kcal, 11.2g protein, 10.5g fat, 1.8g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 128.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 0 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 0.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — eggs have a GI of essentially 0 due to negligible carbohydrate content. The tiny carbohydrate content from dark soy sauce drizzle is negligible. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 166mg per 2-egg serving (including soy sauce drizzle) — about 7% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; eggs themselves are low in sodium. Patients with hypertension may consume half-boiled eggs regularly; the soy sauce drizzle is the primary sodium source and can be omitted or minimised without affecting the nutritional value of the eggs. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Essentially zero glycaemic index; high protein and fat content with no carbohydrates from the eggs themselves; one of the most diabetes-friendly breakfast items available. Half-boiled eggs are an excellent breakfast choice for diabetic patients, providing high-quality protein, healthy fats, and zero glycaemic load; the protein and fat content helps moderate the glycaemic impact of accompanying carbohydrates like kaya toast. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Eggs are a complete protein source with exceptional selenium (30mcg/100g), vitamin D (2.2mcg/100g), and vitamin B12; all three are clinically relevant for immune function and anti-inflammatory pathways in RA. Half-boiled eggs are one of the most nutritionally valuable foods for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the selenium, vitamin D, vitamin B12, and omega-3 fatty acids in egg yolk all support immune regulation and reduce inflammatory burden. Note: Cholesterol content is notably high (365mg/100g for eggs), providing approximately 475mg per 2-egg serving. Current dietary guidelines (2020 Dietary Guidelines) do not set an upper limit for dietary cholesterol for most healthy adults; however, patients with hypercholesterolaemia should discuss egg consumption with their clinician. Selenium content (30mcg/100g) makes eggs one of the best dietary sources of selenium in the Singapore diet.

This is **Tom Yum Soup (clear broth, with prawns)**. Tom Yum Soup (Tom Yum Goong with prawns) is Thailand's most famous soup — a clear, intensely aromatic, hot and sour broth infused with fresh lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and bird's eye chilli, containing whole prawns, straw mushrooms, and tomatoes, seasoned with fish sauce and fresh lime juice. The clear version (tom yum nam sai) is a low-calorie, anti-inflammatory soup with exceptional phytocompound content from the aromatic herbs, while the creamy version (tom yum nam khon) adds coconut milk for a richer, higher-fat profile. It is widely available at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore. Per 100g: 46.0 kcal, 4.2g protein, 1.5g fat, 3.8g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 675.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3038mg per bowl (450g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit; fish sauce is the primary sodium source in the Tom Yum broth. Patients with hypertension should consume Tom Yum Soup in smaller portions, avoid finishing the broth, and ask for reduced fish sauce; the solid components (prawns, mushrooms, vegetables) provide excellent nutrition at a much lower sodium load. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrate content (~3.8g/100g); extremely low calorie density; high protein from prawns with negligible glycaemic impact. Tom Yum Soup is an excellent low-calorie, low-carbohydrate meal option for diabetic patients; pair with a modest portion of steamed rice and the overall glycaemic impact of the meal remains very manageable. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime contain potent anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids; bird's eye chilli provides capsaicin with anti-inflammatory properties. Tom Yum Soup is one of the most anti-inflammatory dishes available; the aromatic herb base (lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime) provides significant anti-inflammatory phytocompound content, making it a beneficial choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Note: Not halal in traditional Thai preparation as fish sauce (nam pla) used by non-certified vendors may not be halal-certified. Sodium is very high from fish sauce — this is the primary nutritional concern. The creamy coconut milk version (tom yum nam khon) has approximately 3× the fat and calorie content.

This is **Nasi Goreng (Indonesian hawker style)**. Nasi Goreng is Indonesia's beloved national fried rice dish — day-old jasmine rice stir-fried in a hot wok with shallots, garlic, and bird's eye chilli, seasoned with kecap manis (sweet Indonesian soy sauce) that gives the rice a characteristic deep brown colour and subtly sweet flavour. The dish is topped with a fried egg and served with cucumber slices, tomato wedges, and often prawn crackers. Unlike Chinese fried rice, Nasi Goreng is defined by the kecap manis sauce and the aromatic shallot-garlic-chilli base, making it sweeter and more fragrant. Per 100g: 173.0 kcal, 5.5g protein, 6.5g fat, 24.2g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 518.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 70 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 59.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (fried); similar GI to plain white rice; kecap manis adds sugar but does not significantly alter the overall GI relative to plain fried rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1813mg per serving (350g) — about 79% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by kecap manis, soy sauce, and seasoning. Patients with hypertension should request reduced kecap manis and soy sauce; nasi goreng contains less sodium than many other hawker rice dishes due to lighter sauce usage compared to Chinese fried rice. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Jasmine rice base has a high GI (~70); kecap manis adds approximately 4.8g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 59 is high. Diabetic patients should limit nasi goreng to a smaller portion (200g) and request reduced kecap manis; the added sugar from the sweet soy sauce compounds the already high glycaemic impact of the rice base. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Shallots and garlic in the stir-fry provide quercetin and allicin with anti-inflammatory effects; the egg provides selenium and vitamin D; overall moderate fat profile. Nasi Goreng is an acceptable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the shallot and garlic base provides anti-inflammatory compounds, and requesting extra vegetables as a side improves the overall anti-inflammatory profile. Note: Halal in standard Indonesian/Malay preparation (no pork). The sugar content from kecap manis is clinically significant for diabetic patients. Prawn crackers accompaniment adds approximately 50–70 kcal per serving if consumed.

This is **Gado Gado (with peanut sauce)**. Gado Gado is an Indonesian mixed vegetable dish of blanched and raw vegetables — typically cabbage, bean sprouts, long beans, spinach, cucumber, and boiled potato — combined with fried tofu, fried tempeh, and hard-boiled egg, all dressed in a rich, spiced peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, tamarind, palm sugar, chilli, garlic, and kaffir lime juice. It is topped with prawn crackers and is one of Indonesia's most nutritionally complete dishes, combining plant protein, diverse vegetables, and healthy fats. Per 100g: 122.0 kcal, 5.5g protein, 7.2g fat, 10.2g carbohydrates, 3.0g dietary fibre, 375.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 40 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 14.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — composite dish GI estimated from vegetable, tofu, and potato components; peanut sauce lowers overall GI through high fat and protein content. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1313mg per serving (350g) — about 57% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by the peanut sauce seasoning and soy-marinated tofu and tempeh. Patients with hypertension should request reduced peanut sauce dressing; the vegetables, tofu, and tempeh themselves are relatively low in sodium, so limiting sauce quantity significantly reduces sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low overall GI (~40) due to high vegetable fibre, tempeh resistant starch, and peanut sauce fat; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 14 is low. Gado Gado is an excellent meal choice for diabetic patients; the high fibre from vegetables, protein from tofu, tempeh, and egg, and the fat content of peanut sauce together produce a low glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Rich in folate, vitamin C, vitamin A, and vitamin E from vegetables; tempeh and tofu provide anti-inflammatory soy isoflavones; peanuts add vitamin E and healthy fats. Gado Gado is an excellent meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the diverse vegetables provide a broad spectrum of anti-inflammatory antioxidants, and tempeh fermentation increases bioavailability of key nutrients. Note: Vegetarian in standard preparation (egg, no meat). Not vegan due to egg and prawn crackers. Peanut allergy is a critical concern. Tempeh provides probiotics and is more nutritionally bioavailable than raw soy. The prawn crackers contain crustaceans and are not accounted for in per_100g values.

This is **Pho Bo (Vietnamese beef noodle soup)**. Pho Bo is Vietnam's most celebrated national dish — a deeply aromatic, crystal-clear beef bone broth simmered for many hours with charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, served over flat rice noodles (banh pho) topped with thinly sliced raw beef (which cooks in the hot broth when poured), braised beef brisket, and finished with fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime, and sliced chilli. The broth's complex spice profile gives pho its distinctive warming, anise-forward fragrance. It is widely available at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 76.0 kcal, 6.2g protein, 2.3g fat, 8.2g carbohydrates, 0.6g dietary fibre, 578.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 45 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 22.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles (flat, cooked); composite GI modified by the high protein content of beef in the broth. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3468mg per bowl (600g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the slow-simmered bone broth concentrates sodium from fish sauce and seasoning. Patients with hypertension should avoid finishing the pho broth and limit additional hoisin sauce or sriracha; the beef and noodles themselves have much lower sodium than the broth. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Flat rice noodles have a moderate GI (~45); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 22 is moderate-low for a full bowl; high protein from beef moderates the glycaemic response significantly. Pho Bo is a relatively diabetes-friendly noodle soup; the high protein from beef slows gastric emptying and moderates glucose absorption from the rice noodles; avoid adding hoisin sauce which contains significant added sugar. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Bone broth provides collagen precursors and glycine; star anise and cinnamon contain anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; beef provides zinc and vitamin B12 supporting immune function. Pho Bo bone broth is traditionally regarded as beneficial for joint health due to its collagen and glycosaminoglycan content; the anti-inflammatory spice profile of the broth (star anise, cinnamon, ginger) provides additional benefit for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Note: Not halal at traditional Vietnamese restaurants due to non-halal-certified fish sauce and potential non-halal beef sourcing. Halal pho is available at halal-certified Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Sodium content is very high from the bone broth — the broth accounts for over 90% of the dish's sodium.

Note: Not halal at traditional Vietnamese restaurants due to non-halal-certified fish sauce and potential non-halal beef sourcing. Halal pho is available at halal-certified Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Sodium content is very high from the bone broth — the broth accounts for over 90% of the dish's sodium.

This is **Pho Bo (Vietnamese beef noodle soup)**. Pho Bo is Vietnam's most celebrated national dish — a deeply aromatic, crystal-clear beef bone broth simmered for many hours with charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, served over flat rice noodles (banh pho) topped with thinly sliced raw beef (which cooks in the hot broth when poured), braised beef brisket, and finished with fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime, and sliced chilli. The broth's complex spice profile gives pho its distinctive warming, anise-forward fragrance. It is widely available at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 76.0 kcal, 6.2g protein, 2.3g fat, 8.2g carbohydrates, 0.6g dietary fibre, 578.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 45 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 22.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles (flat, cooked); composite GI modified by the high protein content of beef in the broth. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3468mg per bowl (600g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the slow-simmered bone broth concentrates sodium from fish sauce and seasoning. Patients with hypertension should avoid finishing the pho broth and limit additional hoisin sauce or sriracha; the beef and noodles themselves have much lower sodium than the broth. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Flat rice noodles have a moderate GI (~45); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 22 is moderate-low for a full bowl; high protein from beef moderates the glycaemic response significantly. Pho Bo is a relatively diabetes-friendly noodle soup; the high protein from beef slows gastric emptying and moderates glucose absorption from the rice noodles; avoid adding hoisin sauce which contains significant added sugar. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Note: Not halal at traditional Vietnamese restaurants due to non-halal-certified fish sauce and potential non-halal beef sourcing. Halal pho is available at halal-certified Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Sodium content is very high from the bone broth — the broth accounts for over 90% of the dish's sodium.

This is **Banh Mi (Vietnamese baguette, pork pâté)**. Bánh Mì is a Vietnamese sandwich that brilliantly fuses French colonial baguette bread with Southeast Asian flavours — a light, crispy-thin Vietnamese baguette filled with pork pâté (chả lụa), char siu pork or Vietnamese cold cuts, pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), sliced cucumber, fresh cilantro, and bird's eye chilli, all drizzled with Maggi seasoning sauce. The Vietnamese baguette is distinguished from the French original by its lighter, airier, crispier shell and more tender crumb, achieved by mixing rice flour into the wheat dough. It is available at Vietnamese bakeries and delis across Singapore. Per 100g: 232.0 kcal, 9.2g protein, 8.2g fat, 30.5g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 518.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 68 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 45.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — baguette (white wheat, French-style); GI elevated by the fine-crumb French-style bread. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1140mg per sandwich (220g) — about 50% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; pork pâté, Maggi seasoning, and pickled vegetables are the primary sodium sources. Patients with hypertension should limit Maggi seasoning and request reduced pâté; the pickled vegetables contribute to sodium but also provide potassium that partially offsets the sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. White baguette has a high GI (~68); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 46 is high; the French-style bread has minimal fibre. Diabetic patients should limit banh mi to an occasional treat and consider requesting wholemeal bread substitutes where available; removing the bread and consuming only the filling dramatically reduces the glycaemic impact. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. White refined flour bread is pro-inflammatory at high consumption; pork pâté is high in saturated fat; pickled vegetables provide probiotic benefit. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit white bread consumption including banh mi; the pickled vegetable component provides probiotic and antioxidant benefit, and cilantro provides anti-inflammatory compounds. Note: Not halal due to pork pâté. Halal versions with chicken or beef are available at some Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The Vietnamese baguette has a lower density than a French baguette, resulting in fewer calories per cm than expected. Iron content is notable from pork pâté.

This is **Ondeh Ondeh (pandan glutinous rice, palm sugar)**. Ondeh Ondeh (Onde Onde) is a beloved Peranakan and Malay kueh (sweet cake) of small glutinous rice flour balls dyed vivid green with fresh pandan juice, filled with a nugget of gula melaka (palm sugar) that dissolves to a liquid when cooked, and rolled in freshly grated coconut. When bitten, the ball releases a burst of caramel-sweet palm sugar juice. They are one of the most visually striking and flavour-forward kueh in Singapore's traditional confectionery repertoire, sold at Peranakan cake stalls, wet markets, and traditional kueh shops. Per 100g: 218.0 kcal, 2.5g protein, 5.2g fat, 41.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 82.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 78 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 32.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — glutinous rice (cooked); GI very high due to the waxy starch structure of glutinous rice flour and the high sugar content from gula melaka. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 82mg per 4-piece serving (100g) — about 4% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; Ondeh Ondeh is one of the lowest-sodium kueh in Singapore. Patients with hypertension may consume Ondeh Ondeh occasionally; the sodium content is negligible, though the high sugar content warrants attention for overall metabolic health. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Glutinous rice flour has a very high GI (~78); the gula melaka filling adds 18.5g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 32 from just 4 small pieces is high. Diabetic patients should strictly limit Ondeh Ondeh consumption; the combination of very high GI glutinous rice and high sugar from palm sugar filling produces a significant postprandial glucose spike; 1–2 pieces maximum as an occasional treat. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; pandan provides chlorophyll antioxidants; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Ondeh Ondeh to occasional treats; the high sugar and refined glutinous rice content are pro-inflammatory at frequent consumption. Note: Fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free in the standard preparation. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. Sugar content from gula melaka is the primary nutritional concern. The coconut coating adds medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Pandan provides trace chlorophyll and phytol antioxidants.

This is **Ang Ku Kueh (groundnut filling)**. Ang Ku Kueh (literally 'red tortoise cake') is a traditional Hokkien-Chinese kueh of a bright red glutinous rice and sweet potato dough moulded in a wooden press into the shape of a tortoise shell and filled with sweetened ground peanut paste, then steamed on banana leaf. The red colour from food colouring and the tortoise shape symbolise longevity and prosperity in Chinese culture, making this kueh an important offering at religious ceremonies, birthdays, and the One Month celebration of newborns. Per 100g: 265.0 kcal, 5.2g protein, 6.8g fat, 46.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 118.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 75 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 34.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — glutinous rice (cooked); GI very high from waxy glutinous rice starch; peanut filling slightly moderates overall GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 118mg per 2-piece serving (100g) — about 5% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; a very low-sodium traditional kueh. Patients with hypertension may consume Ang Ku Kueh occasionally; the sodium content is negligible. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Glutinous rice flour has a very high GI (~75); high sugar content from filling (18.2g/100g); glycaemic load of approximately 35 per 2-piece serving is high. Diabetic patients should strictly limit Ang Ku Kueh to 1 piece maximum as an occasional treat; the glutinous rice base has one of the highest GIs of all carbohydrate foods. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High glycaemic refined carbohydrate base may promote inflammation at regular consumption; peanuts provide vitamin E and healthy fats with mild anti-inflammatory properties. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit ang ku kueh to occasional consumption; the high sugar and glutinous rice content can promote glycaemic-driven inflammation. Note: Fully vegan and gluten-free. Peanut allergy is a critical concern. The mung bean or lotus paste versions will have different nutritional profiles. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. The red food colouring adds no measurable nutrients.

This is **Putu Piring (steamed rice cake, palm sugar)**. Putu Piring is a traditional Malay steamed kueh of lightly fragrant coarse rice flour pressed into small round moulds with a nugget of gula melaka (palm sugar) hidden in the centre, then steamed until the rice flour sets into a soft, slightly granular cake. When bitten, the molten gula melaka filling flows out, imparting a deep, caramel-sweet, slightly smoky palm sugar flavour. The cakes are served on banana leaf with freshly grated coconut and eaten immediately while warm. Putu Piring is associated with traditional Malay kueh stalls and is one of Singapore's most nostalgic street foods. Per 100g: 192.0 kcal, 2.2g protein, 3.5g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 65.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 28.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — coarse rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch; gula melaka adds significant sugar. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 65mg per 4-piece serving (100g) — about 3% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; Putu Piring is one of the lowest-sodium kueh available. Patients with hypertension may consume Putu Piring occasionally without sodium concerns; the very low sodium makes it appropriate as an occasional sweet treat. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Rice flour base has a high GI (~72); gula melaka provides 16.8g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 28 is significant. Diabetic patients should limit Putu Piring to 1–2 pieces maximum as an occasional treat; the high GI rice flour base and palm sugar filling together produce a significant postprandial glucose rise. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. This is **Kueh Tutu (coconut / groundnut filling)**. Kueh Tutu is one of Singapore's most traditional and nostalgic street kueh — small, white dome-shaped steamed cakes made from finely ground rice flour pressed into metal mould plates with a sweet filling of either freshly shredded coconut with sugar, or ground roasted peanuts with sugar. The moulds are placed over a steamer and the kueh is ready in minutes, served fresh in a small banana or pandan leaf wrapper. Kueh Tutu stalls, once common throughout Singapore, are now rare heritage fixtures, making them a sought-after nostalgic treat at cultural events and selected hawker centres. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 5.2g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 78.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 25.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fine rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch similar to putu piring. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 70mg per 3-piece serving (90g) — about 3% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lowest-sodium traditional kueh. Patients with hypertension may consume Kueh Tutu occasionally; the sodium content is negligible. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Fine rice flour base has a high GI (~72); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 26 from just 3 pieces is significant; sugar from filling adds further glycaemic impact. Diabetic patients should limit Kueh Tutu to 1–2 pieces as an occasional treat; the high GI rice flour base and sweet filling together produce a significant postprandial glucose rise disproportionate to the small portion size. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kueh Tutu to occasional treats due to the high sugar and refined rice flour content. Note: Coconut version is listed here. Groundnut version has slightly higher protein (5.5g/100g), higher fat (8.5g/100g), and lower saturated fat ratio. Both versions are fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free (coconut version). This is the final record in Batch 1 of the Project Veritas initiative.

Kueh Tutu is one of Singapore's most traditional and nostalgic street kueh — small, white dome-shaped steamed cakes made from finely ground rice flour pressed into metal mould plates with a sweet filling of either freshly shredded coconut with sugar, or ground roasted peanuts with sugar. The moulds are placed over a steamer and the kueh is ready in minutes, served fresh in a small banana or pandan leaf wrapper. Kueh Tutu stalls, once common throughout Singapore, are now rare heritage fixtures, making them a sought-after nostalgic treat at cultural events and selected hawker centres. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 5.2g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 78.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 25.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fine rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch similar to putu piring. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 70mg per 3-piece serving (90g) — about 3% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lowest-sodium traditional kueh. Patients with hypertension may consume Kueh Tutu occasionally; the sodium content is negligible. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Fine rice flour base has a high GI (~72); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 26 from just 3 pieces is significant; sugar from filling adds further glycaemic impact. Diabetic patients should limit Kueh Tutu to 1–2 pieces as an occasional treat; the high GI rice flour base and sweet filling together produce a significant postprandial glucose rise disproportionate to the small portion size. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kueh Tutu to occasional treats due to the high sugar and refined rice flour content. Note: Coconut version is listed here. Groundnut version has slightly higher protein (5.5g/100g), higher fat (8.5g/100g), and lower saturated fat ratio. Both versions are fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free (coconut version). This is the final record in Batch 1 of the Project Veritas initiative.

This is **Medu Vada**. Medu Vada is one of South India's most iconic snacks — a savoury, crispy, ring-shaped fritter made from ground urad dal (dehusked black lentils) batter seasoned with cumin, black pepper, green chilli, and curry leaves. The doughnut-shaped vada is deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crunchy while the interior remains soft. High in plant protein and iron, but calorie-dense due to deep frying. In Singapore, medu vada is available at South Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and tiffin counters, always served as a pair with sambar and coconut chutney. Per 100g: 322.0 kcal, 10.2g protein, 17.5g fat, 34.8g carbohydrates, 3.8g dietary fibre, 285.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 42 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 17.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — urad dal-based product, estimated from whole urad dal GI; deep-frying and whole lentil structure significantly lower GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Deep-fried preparation raises fat content significantly; 2-piece serving (120g) provides approximately 21g fat and 342mg sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit medu vada to 1–2 pieces and avoid pairing with high-sodium sambar. The deep-frying oil quality also matters for cardiovascular health. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low GI (~42) due to the protein and fibre content of urad dal and structural resistance of deep-fried batter; glycaemic load per serving is moderate. Despite deep-frying, medu vada has a low glycaemic index and can be consumed occasionally by diabetic patients. Limit to 1–2 pieces and monitor the sambar and chutney accompaniments for added carbohydrates. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Deep-fried in vegetable oil which, if refined and reused, contributes to pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acids; urad dal itself is an excellent anti-inflammatory legume. Urad dal is rich in protein and folate which support immune regulation. Consuming occasionally is acceptable; avoid if fried in highly re-used oil. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Cholesterol is 0.0 as urad dal contains no cholesterol. Sambar and coconut chutney are not included in per_100g values — these are for the vada alone. Calorie density is driven by the deep-frying oil absorbed (~17.5g fat per 100g).

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Uttapam**. Uttapam is a thick, savoury South Indian pancake made from the same fermented batter of parboiled rice and urad dal used for dosa and idli, but cooked much thicker (0.5–1cm) on a flat griddle with toppings pressed into the surface before cooking. The standard toppings include diced onion, tomato, green chilli, and fresh coriander. It is sometimes called 'Indian pizza' for its thick-base appearance. Compared to plain dosa, uttapam is higher in fat (from griddle oil) but offers more vegetable content and fibre from the toppings. In Singapore, uttapam is a staple at South Indian tiffin counters and banana-leaf restaurants. Per 100g: 180.0 kcal, 5.2g protein, 4.8g fat, 30.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 215.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 68 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 37.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fermented rice-lentil batter pancakes; thick batter and toppings moderate the GI slightly vs plain dosa. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 387mg per piece (180g) — moderate; the vegetable toppings add minimal sodium while increasing potassium content. Suitable for hypertensive patients when consumed with sambar and chutney in moderation; the vegetable toppings enhance nutrient density. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~68) — lower than plain idli due to the thick batter and vegetable topping adding fibre; glycaemic load per full piece is approximately 37. Patients with diabetes should limit to half a piece (90g) per meal or choose ragi (finger millet) uttapam for a lower-GI alternative. The vegetable toppings improve the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Fermented batter supports gut microbiome health; fresh tomato and coriander toppings provide antioxidants and vitamin C. Uttapam is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — gluten-free, fermented, and enriched with anti-inflammatory vegetable toppings. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Vitamin C comes from tomato and green chilli toppings. Cholesterol is 0.0. The sambar and chutney accompaniments are not included in per_100g values.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Appam**. Appam is a beautifully distinctive South Indian — particularly Kerala — pancake made from fermented rice batter blended with coconut milk. Cooked in a small, rounded wok called an appachatti, the batter spreads to create a circular pancake with thin, lacy, crispy edges and a thick, soft, spongy centre. The coconut milk gives appam a subtle sweetness and rich aroma. It is gluten-free and vegan. In Singapore, appam is found at South Indian and Sri Lankan restaurants and is typically served with sweetened coconut milk, vegetable stew (ishtew), or egg. Per 100g: 164.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 4.5g fat, 28.5g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 175.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 76 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 34.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fermented rice pancake category; GI is high due to white rice starch but fat from coconut milk moderates somewhat. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 263mg per 2-piece serving (150g) — moderate; coconut milk adds no sodium naturally. Suitable for hypertensive patients. Appam is naturally low in sodium. The accompaniments (sweetened coconut milk, vegetable stew) add minimal sodium compared to curry-based accompaniments. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. High GI (~76) due to gelatinised white rice starch; glycaemic load per 2-piece serving is approximately 34. Diabetic patients should limit appam to 1 piece and pair with egg or vegetable stew to increase protein content; avoid sweetened coconut milk accompaniment which adds further sugar. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Coconut milk provides medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with some evidence for anti-inflammatory effects; fermentation supports gut health. Appam is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The saturated fat from coconut should not be a concern at standard serving levels. Note: Vegan and gluten-free. Coconut is classified as a tree nut allergen. Cholesterol is 0.0. The high saturated fat per 100g (~3.8g) is entirely from coconut milk — medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs). Accompaniments are not included.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Pongal**. Pongal (specifically Ven Pongal or Khara Pongal) is a beloved South Indian comfort dish from Tamil Nadu — a soft, creamy, savoury porridge made by pressure-cooking white rice and split yellow moong dal together until the mixture is completely amalgamated and mushy. It is generously flavoured with a hot ghee tempering of cumin seeds, cracked black pepper, fresh ginger, curry leaves, and golden-fried cashews. The dish is soft, warming, mildly spiced, and mildly nutty. In Singapore, pongal is a breakfast staple at South Indian restaurants and is served with sambar and coconut chutney. Per 100g: 148.0 kcal, 4.5g protein, 4.8g fat, 22.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 188.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 40.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked white rice and moong dal mixture; moong dal significantly lowers GI vs plain rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 470mg per bowl (250g) — moderate; pongal is a naturally low-sodium dish and the sodium comes from added salt which can be reduced. Suitable for hypertensive patients when prepared with reduced salt; avoid high-sodium sambar accompaniment for strict sodium restriction. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate-high GI (~72); glycaemic load per full bowl (250g) is approximately 40.5 — high; the moong dal provides fibre and protein which moderates the spike vs plain rice. Patients with diabetes should limit to a 150g portion and pair with a protein-rich side. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre which helps moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins; black pepper and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties; moong dal is a low-purine legume. Pongal is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — ghee, ginger, and black pepper are traditional Ayurvedic anti-inflammatory ingredients. Note: Contains ghee (dairy/milk) and cashew (tree nut) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 26mg per bowl (250g) from ghee. Sweet sakkarai pongal (jaggery pongal) is a different dish with significantly higher sugar content — values here are for savoury ven pongal only.

Pongal (specifically Ven Pongal or Khara Pongal) is a beloved South Indian comfort dish from Tamil Nadu — a soft, creamy, savoury porridge made by pressure-cooking white rice and split yellow moong dal together until the mixture is completely amalgamated and mushy. It is generously flavoured with a hot ghee tempering of cumin seeds, cracked black pepper, fresh ginger, curry leaves, and golden-fried cashews. The dish is soft, warming, mildly spiced, and mildly nutty. In Singapore, pongal is a breakfast staple at South Indian restaurants and is served with sambar and coconut chutney. Per 100g: 148.0 kcal, 4.5g protein, 4.8g fat, 22.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 188.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 40.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked white rice and moong dal mixture; moong dal significantly lowers GI vs plain rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 470mg per bowl (250g) — moderate; pongal is a naturally low-sodium dish and the sodium comes from added salt which can be reduced. Suitable for hypertensive patients when prepared with reduced salt; avoid high-sodium sambar accompaniment for strict sodium restriction. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate-high GI (~72); glycaemic load per full bowl (250g) is approximately 40.5 — high; the moong dal provides fibre and protein which moderates the spike vs plain rice. Patients with diabetes should limit to a 150g portion and pair with a protein-rich side. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre which helps moderate the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins; black pepper and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties; moong dal is a low-purine legume. Pongal is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — ghee, ginger, and black pepper are traditional Ayurvedic anti-inflammatory ingredients. Note: Contains ghee (dairy/milk) and cashew (tree nut) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 26mg per bowl (250g) from ghee. Sweet sakkarai pongal (jaggery pongal) is a different dish with significantly higher sugar content — values here are for savoury ven pongal only.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Upma**. Upma is a popular South Indian breakfast porridge made by dry-roasting semolina (rava/sooji) until fragrant, then adding it to a tempered base of oil, mustard seeds, urad dal, onion, green chilli, ginger, and curry leaves, and cooking with water to a smooth, thick consistency. The texture is fluffy and slightly grainy, with bursts of flavour from the tempering. Common add-ins include peas, cashews, and grated coconut. It is quick to prepare and widely available at South Indian tiffin counters across Singapore. Note: upma contains gluten as it is made from wheat semolina. Per 100g: 145.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 4.5g fat, 23.5g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 200.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 66 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 38.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from semolina (rava) cooked; rava has a moderate GI and the tempering adds fat which slightly lowers GI vs plain boiled semolina. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 500mg per bowl (250g) — moderate; most sodium is from added salt during cooking which can be reduced. Suitable for hypertensive patients when prepared with reduced salt. Upma is a naturally low-saturated-fat, low-cholesterol dish. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~66); glycaemic load per full bowl is approximately 39. Semolina has a higher GI than oats but lower than refined white rice. Diabetic patients should limit to 150g and prefer rava upma made with durum wheat semolina over maida-based variants. Adding vegetables (peas, carrot) increases fibre and lowers glycaemic load. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Contains mustard seeds and ginger which have anti-inflammatory properties; semolina provides B vitamins including selenium. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Not suitable for patients with gluten intolerance (contains wheat semolina). Ghee-based upma provides butyrate benefits. Note: Contains wheat gluten (semolina) — not suitable for coeliac or gluten-sensitive patients. Vegan when made with oil; vegetarian when made with ghee (ghee version adds cholesterol ~8mg/100g and dairy allergen). Cholesterol value of 0.0 assumes oil-based preparation.
