content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chole Bhature**. Chole Bhature is a legendary North Indian street food and comfort meal — two inseparable components served together: Chole, a dark, intensely spiced chickpea curry with a dry-ish tomato-onion gravy flavoured with dried pomegranate seed powder and whole spices; and Bhature, large, puffed, golden, deep-fried leavened bread made from refined flour and yogurt. One of the most indulgent and calorie-dense dishes in Indian cuisine, chole bhature is a beloved breakfast-to-lunch item in Delhi and Punjab. The chickpea provides outstanding nutrition (high fibre, protein, folate) but the deep-fried bhatura adds significant calories and fat. Per 100g: 265.0 kcal, 7.8g protein, 12.5g fat, 34.5g carbohydrates, 4.2g dietary fibre, 352.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 75.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from combined chickpea curry (low GI) and deep-fried wheat bhatura (high GI); the combined GI is moderate but the glycaemic load per full serving is very high. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1232mg per full serving (350g) — very high; the spice blend, salt in chole, and baking soda in bhatura all contribute. Patients with hypertension should treat chole bhature as an occasional indulgence meal. Limit to half a serving (175g). The high potassium from chickpeas provides some offset. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~62) but very high glycaemic load per full serving (350g) of approximately 75 — one of the highest glycaemic loads of any standard single meal. Diabetic patients should avoid chole bhature as a regular meal. The deep-fried bhatura has a very high glycaemic load. If consumed, limit to a quarter bhatura with 100g of chole. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Deep-frying oil from bhatura contributes omega-6 fatty acids; chickpeas are highly anti-inflammatory but the bhatura's refined flour and frying offset this. Consume occasionally. The chickpea chole is anti-inflammatory and high in folate, but the deep-fried bhatura undermines the overall nutritional benefit. Prefer the chole alone with chapati. Note: Contains gluten (bhatura is made from refined wheat flour) and dairy (yogurt in bhatura dough). Not vegan or suitable for gluten-sensitive patients. Per_100g values are for the combined chole + bhatura serving (350g total). Cholesterol approximately 8.75mg per full serving — primarily from yogurt in bhatura. Very high glycaemic load per full serving (approximately 75).

This is **Vegetable Pakora**. Vegetable Pakora is a classic Indian snack — sliced or diced mixed vegetables (onion, potato, spinach, green chilli, cauliflower) coated in a thick, spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter seasoned with ajwain (carom seeds), red chilli, and coriander, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The chickpea flour batter creates a distinctive, slightly nutty and crispy coating. Pakoras are universally available at Indian restaurants in Singapore as a starter or snack. Despite the deep-frying, the chickpea flour base gives pakoras a surprisingly low glycaemic index. Per 100g: 295.0 kcal, 6.2g protein, 17.5g fat, 31.5g carbohydrates, 4.2g dietary fibre, 285.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 38 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 17.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from chickpea-battered deep-fried vegetable; chickpea flour has a low GI (~28) which significantly moderates the overall GI despite deep-frying. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 428mg per 6-piece serving (150g) — moderate; salt in batter and mint chutney dipping sauce contribute to sodium. content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Dhokla**. Dhokla is a beloved Gujarati snack — a spongy, light, soft steamed cake made from a fermented batter of chickpea flour (besan) and yogurt, leavened with baking soda or Eno. After steaming in a shallow tray, it is cut into diamond or square shapes and finished with a hot oil tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chilli, and garnished with grated coconut and coriander. The flavour is pleasantly tangy from the yogurt and lemon, mildly sweet from the batter, and fragrant from the tempering. Dhokla is gluten-free, low in fat (steamed, not fried), and has a low GI. Available at Indian sweet shops and restaurants across Singapore. Per 100g: 155.0 kcal, 6.5g protein, 3.2g fat, 26.5g carbohydrates, 3.2g dietary fibre, 310.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 35 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 13.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from steamed chickpea flour preparation; chickpea flour base and fermentation give a low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 465mg per 4-piece serving (150g) — moderate to elevated; baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) contributes significantly to sodium content. Patients with hypertension should limit to 2–3 pieces. The baking soda used for leavening is a significant sodium source. Home preparation allows reduction of leavening agent sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low GI (~35) from chickpea flour; glycaemic load per 4-piece serving is approximately 14 — low. The fermentation and chickpea base give excellent glycaemic control. Excellent snack for diabetic patients — low GI and moderate glycaemic load. Chickpea flour is one of the best flours for blood glucose management. The added sugar (for tempering/sweetening) is small. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Steaming (not frying) preserves the anti-inflammatory properties; chickpea flour provides folate; turmeric provides curcumin; fermentation supports gut microbiome. Excellent snack for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — steamed preparation, chickpea-based, fermented, and flavoured with anti-inflammatory turmeric. Low in saturated fat and inflammatory fats. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt) and legume (chickpea) allergens. Gluten-free — no wheat flour. Cholesterol approximately 3.75mg per serving (150g) from yogurt. The sugar in the batter (approximately 5.5g/100g) is from the added sugar which sweetens the dhokla slightly. Baking soda is a significant sodium source — reduce or substitute with Eno for lower sodium version.

The flavour is pleasantly tangy from the yogurt and lemon, mildly sweet from the batter, and fragrant from the tempering. Dhokla is gluten-free, low in fat (steamed, not fried), and has a low GI. Available at Indian sweet shops and restaurants across Singapore. Per 100g: 155.0 kcal, 6.5g protein, 3.2g fat, 26.5g carbohydrates, 3.2g dietary fibre, 310.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 35 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 13.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from steamed chickpea flour preparation; chickpea flour base and fermentation give a low GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 465mg per 4-piece serving (150g) — moderate to elevated; baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) contributes significantly to sodium content. Patients with hypertension should limit to 2–3 pieces. The baking soda used for leavening is a significant sodium source. Home preparation allows reduction of leavening agent sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low GI (~35) from chickpea flour; glycaemic load per 4-piece serving is approximately 14 — low. The fermentation and chickpea base give excellent glycaemic control. Excellent snack for diabetic patients — low GI and moderate glycaemic load. Chickpea flour is one of the best flours for blood glucose management. The added sugar (for tempering/sweetening) is small. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Steaming (not frying) preserves the anti-inflammatory properties; chickpea flour provides folate; turmeric provides curcumin; fermentation supports gut microbiome. Excellent snack for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — steamed preparation, chickpea-based, fermented, and flavoured with anti-inflammatory turmeric. Low in saturated fat and inflammatory fats. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt) and legume (chickpea) allergens. Gluten-free — no wheat flour. Cholesterol approximately 3.75mg per serving (150g) from yogurt. The sugar in the batter (approximately 5.5g/100g) is from the added sugar which sweetens the dhokla slightly. Baking soda is a significant sodium source — reduce or substitute with Eno for lower sodium version.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Murukku**. Murukku is a classic South Indian deep-fried snack — a dough of rice flour and urad dal flour seasoned with cumin seeds, sesame seeds, butter, and asafoetida, pressed through a star-shaped mould directly into hot oil and fried until golden-brown and remarkably hard and crunchy. The distinctive spiral or circular shape breaks with a satisfying crunch. It has a savoury, slightly nutty, cumin-sesame flavour and is gluten-free and vegan (when made with oil, not butter). In Singapore, murukku is a staple snack at Deepavali (Diwali) celebrations, at Indian sweet shops year-round, and at hawker centres. Per 100g: 478.0 kcal, 6.5g protein, 25.5g fat, 58.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 425.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 213mg per 50g serving — moderate; murukku is a salty snack and the sodium per serving accumulates quickly if multiple servings are consumed. Patients with hypertension should limit murukku to 25–30g (2–3 spirals) per occasion. It is easy to overconsume as a festive snack. Choose reduced-salt varieties where available. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Very high caloric density (478 kcal/100g); rice flour base is moderate-high GI; the high fat from deep frying blunts the glycaemic response somewhat. Diabetic patients should limit murukku to 2–3 spirals (20–25g) on special occasions. The very high caloric density means even small portions contribute significant energy. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Deep-fried in oil with high omega-6 content; urad dal provides folate which supports immune function; cumin and sesame seeds have anti-inflammatory properties. Limit murukku to occasional festive consumption. The deep-frying method and high omega-6 fat content may exacerbate inflammation with regular consumption. The small standard serving (50g) makes it acceptable as an occasional treat. Note: Contains dairy (butter) when made with butter; vegan and dairy-free when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 2.5mg per 50g serving when made with butter. GI and GL are null — insufficient published data. Very low water content (~3.5%) as murukku is a dry, hard fried snack. Calorie density is very high — weight-for-weight one of the most energy-dense Indian snacks.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Mutton Curry (Indian)**. Mutton Curry (Indian) is a robust, hearty, aromatic curry of bone-in mutton (goat) pieces pressure-cooked with onion, tomato, ginger-garlic paste, yogurt, and a full South Asian spice blend of coriander, cumin, red chilli, garam masala, and turmeric. The gravy is medium-thick and dark-reddish, concentrated from the pressure-cooking. The bone-in preparation gives the curry a rich, deep flavour from the bone marrow. Very high in zinc and vitamin B12 — two nutrients critical for immune function. Available at South Indian and Indian Muslim restaurants across Singapore. Per 100g: 165.0 kcal, 14.5g protein, 10.8g fat, 4.5g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 385.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 963mg per bowl (250g) — high; the curry gravy contains significant salt and the pressure-cooking concentrates sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit mutton curry to a small portion (100–150g) and reduce salt during cooking. The high zinc and potassium content is somewhat beneficial. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Negligible carbohydrate content; GI testing not applicable. High protein (14.5g/100g) and fat provide satiety without a glycaemic response. Mutton curry has no direct glycaemic impact. However, the high fat content means patients should monitor total caloric intake. Pair with a low-GI grain (basmati rice or chapati) for a complete meal. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Medium. Mutton is a red meat high in arachidonic acid (a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids); however it provides exceptional zinc (4.5mg/100g), B12, and selenium which support immune function. Limit to 1–2 servings per week. The zinc, selenium, and vitamin B12 from mutton are highly beneficial for immune regulation. However, the red meat and saturated fat content warrants moderation for patients with active inflammation. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt) at trace levels from marinating. GI and GL are null — negligible carbohydrate content. Cholesterol approximately 180mg per bowl (250g). Zinc is exceptionally high (4.5mg/100g) — one of the richest zinc sources in Indian cuisine. Per_100g values include both meat and gravy in standard proportion.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Egg Curry**. Egg Curry is a widely enjoyed Indian dish — hard-boiled eggs (often first shallow-fried in oil to create a golden, slightly crispy surface) simmered in a medium-thick onion-tomato gravy spiced with cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala. It is a simple, economical, and nutritionally excellent curry available at virtually all South Indian and Indian restaurants in Singapore. Rich in high-quality protein, vitamins B2, B12, D, and selenium from the eggs, combined with the anti-inflammatory spices from the gravy. Per 100g: 148.0 kcal, 8.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 6.5g carbohydrates, 1.2g dietary fibre, 318.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Medium. Sodium approximately 636mg per 2-egg serving (200g) — elevated; the gravy and salt from cooking concentrate sodium significantly. Patients with hypertension should reduce added salt in the gravy and limit portion size. The egg itself is a good source of potassium and magnesium for blood pressure support. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrates; GI testing not applicable. High protein (8.5g/100g) from eggs promotes satiety and blood glucose stability. Egg curry is an excellent choice for diabetic patients — low in carbohydrates, high in protein, and moderate in fat. Eggs provide a low GI protein source that does not raise blood glucose. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Eggs are one of the best dietary sources of selenium (22mcg/100g) and vitamin D (1.5mcg/100g) — both critical for immune function; turmeric provides curcumin. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the selenium and vitamin D from eggs support immune regulation and bone health. The turmeric in the gravy provides anti-inflammatory curcumin. Note: Contains eggs — not vegan. Suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians. Cholesterol is high (195mg/100g, approximately 390mg per 2-egg serving) from the egg yolks. GI and GL are null — negligible carbohydrate content. Vitamin D (1.5mcg/100g) makes egg curry one of the few non-fish Indian dishes with meaningful vitamin D content.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Gulab Jamun**. Gulab Jamun is one of India's most iconic and beloved desserts — small, round balls made from khoya (reduced evaporated milk solids) mixed with a little flour and leavening, deep-fried until uniformly dark golden-brown, then soaked in warm sugar syrup flavoured with rosewater and cardamom until they absorb the syrup and become plump, soft, and glistening. The texture is uniquely spongy and the flavour intensely sweet and milky with a floral rose note. Rich in calcium from the khoya but very high in sugar. Served at celebrations, festivals, and as a dessert at Indian restaurants in Singapore. Per 100g: 342.0 kcal, 5.5g protein, 12.5g fat, 55.5g carbohydrates, 0.2g dietary fibre, 88.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 76 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 42.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from dairy-based fried confection soaked in sugar syrup; high sugar content from syrup gives a very high GI and glycaemic load. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 88mg per 100g serving — low; gulab jamun is naturally low in sodium. The sugar and fat content are the primary health concerns, not sodium. From a sodium standpoint, gulab jamun is suitable for hypertensive patients. The concern is the very high sugar and calorie content from the sugar syrup. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Very high GI (~76) and very high sugar content (48.5g/100g — primarily from sugar syrup); glycaemic load per 2-piece serving (100g) is approximately 42. Gulab jamun must be avoided by diabetic patients — it is one of the highest GI and highest glycaemic load desserts available. Even a single piece (50g) provides ~24g of rapidly absorbed sugar. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: High. Very high sugar content (48.5g/100g) promotes inflammatory cytokine production; deep-frying adds pro-inflammatory fats; minimal anti-inflammatory nutrients. Gulab jamun should be consumed very sparingly by patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the high sugar content and deep-frying combination are strongly pro-inflammatory. Limit to 1 piece on rare festive occasions. Note: Contains dairy (khoya/milk) and gluten (maida). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 28mg per 100g serving. The sugar syrup accounts for the majority (48.5g/100g) of the carbohydrate content. High caloric density (342 kcal per 100g) is driven by sugar syrup absorption and fat from frying.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Jalebi**. Jalebi is one of India's most vibrant street food sweets — a fermented wheat flour batter piped through a cloth or squeeze bottle in spiral shapes directly into hot oil, deep-fried until crispy, then immediately immersed in warm sugar syrup flavoured with saffron and cardamom until they absorb the syrup and become glisteningly orange and syrup-saturated. The contrast between the crispy exterior and syrup-filled interior is the defining eating experience. Very high in sugar — the syrup penetrates the fried dough completely. In Singapore, jalebi is sold at Indian sweet shops and during Deepavali celebrations. Per 100g: 380.0 kcal, 3.2g protein, 12.8g fat, 65.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 48.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 82 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 53.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — jalebi GI very high due to the predominantly sugar syrup composition; almost entirely rapidly-absorbed simple sugars. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 48mg per 100g serving — very low; jalebi is essentially sugar, oil, and flour with minimal salt. From a sodium standpoint, jalebi is safe for hypertensive patients. The primary health concern is the very high sugar content. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Very high GI (~82) and very high sugar content (52g/100g); glycaemic load per 100g serving is approximately 54 — extremely high. Jalebi is among the most glycaemically dangerous foods. Diabetic patients must avoid jalebi completely or limit to 1 small piece (15–20g) on rare occasions under glucose monitoring. The sugar syrup content is essentially pure rapidly-absorbed sucrose. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: High. Very high sugar content (52g/100g) promotes inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); deep frying adds pro-inflammatory omega-6 fats. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit jalebi — the combination of extremely high sugar and deep-frying is among the most pro-inflammatory dietary patterns. Occasional small piece on festive occasions only. Note: Contains gluten (wheat flour) and dairy (yogurt at trace fermentation level). Not suitable for coeliac or gluten-sensitive patients. Cholesterol approximately 2mg per 100g from trace yogurt. The very high sugar (52g/100g) is primarily from sugar syrup absorption. Very low water content (16.5%) reflects the high sugar and fat density. Imarti (made with urad dal instead of wheat) is a gluten-free variant with similar caloric profile.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Kheer**. Kheer is India's beloved national dessert — a creamy, sweet rice pudding made by slowly simmering rice in full-fat milk on low heat for 40–60 minutes, stirring frequently, until the milk reduces and thickens and the rice becomes very soft. Sweetened with sugar and perfumed with cardamom, saffron, and rosewater, then garnished with slivered cashews, almonds, pistachios, and raisins. It is served warm, at room temperature, or chilled. Rich in calcium and vitamin B12 from dairy. The defining difference from payasam (#096) is that kheer uses cow's milk only, while payasam uses coconut milk and/or vermicelli. Per 100g: 182.0 kcal, 4.5g protein, 6.8g fat, 28.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 62.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 27.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from rice pudding in full-fat milk; the milk fat and protein moderate the GI of the rice; GI lower than plain cooked rice. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 93mg per bowl (150g) — very low. Kheer is naturally low in sodium; the calcium content (138mg/100g) is beneficial for blood pressure regulation. Suitable for hypertensive patients from a sodium standpoint. The concern is the moderate sugar content (18.5g/100g). Reduced-sugar kheer is an option. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~65); glycaemic load per bowl (150g) is approximately 28. The sugar content (18.5g/100g) is the primary concern. Diabetic patients should limit kheer to half a serving (75g) or choose a reduced-sugar version made with natural sweeteners. The milk protein and fat moderate the GI compared to plain rice. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Calcium (138mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (0.38mcg/100g) from milk are beneficial for bone health; cardamom and saffron have anti-inflammatory properties. Suitable in moderation for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The calcium and vitamin B12 support bone and immune health. The moderate sugar content is a consideration for frequent consumption. Note: Contains dairy (milk, cream) and tree nuts (cashew, almond, pistachio). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 27mg per bowl (150g). Vitamin B12 is well-represented from full-fat milk. The calorie and sugar content vary significantly based on milk-to-rice ratio and amount of sugar added.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Payasam (Semiya)**. Semiya Payasam is a classic South Indian festival dessert — thin wheat vermicelli (semiya) toasted in ghee until golden, then simmered in a mixture of full-fat cow's milk and coconut milk, sweetened with sugar, and flavoured with cardamom and saffron. Finished with ghee-fried cashews and raisins. The result is a creamy, fragrant, slightly thick dessert soup served warm or at room temperature. Payasam differs from North Indian kheer in using coconut milk and wheat vermicelli rather than pure cow's milk and rice. A traditional Onam Sadya and festive offering in South India and Singapore's Tamil community. Per 100g: 195.0 kcal, 4.2g protein, 8.5g fat, 28.5g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 55.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 26.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from wheat vermicelli in full-fat coconut milk and cow's milk; coconut milk fat and cow's milk protein moderate the GI of the vermicelli. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 83mg per bowl (150g) — very low. Payasam is naturally very low in sodium. Suitable for hypertensive patients from a sodium standpoint. The moderate sugar and fat content are the primary dietary considerations. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~62); glycaemic load per bowl (150g) is approximately 27. The sugar content (16.5g/100g) combined with wheat vermicelli creates a significant glycaemic load. Diabetic patients should limit payasam to half a serving (75g) or choose a reduced-sugar version. The coconut milk fat moderates the GI somewhat but the sugar content is a significant concern. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Coconut milk provides lauric acid with anti-inflammatory properties; cardamom and saffron have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; milk provides calcium. Suitable in moderation for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The coconut milk and spice content provide some anti-inflammatory benefit. Contains gluten (wheat vermicelli) — not suitable for coeliac patients. Note: Contains gluten (wheat vermicelli), dairy (milk, ghee), and tree nuts (cashew). Not vegan, not gluten-free, not dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 18mg per bowl (150g). Key distinction from Kheer (#095): payasam uses coconut milk (higher saturated fat), wheat vermicelli (not rice), and the texture is slightly looser. Higher saturated fat than kheer due to coconut milk MCTs.

This is **Payasam (Semiya)**. Semiya Payasam is a classic South Indian festival dessert — thin wheat vermicelli (semiya) toasted in ghee until golden, then simmered in a mixture of full-fat cow's milk and coconut milk, sweetened with sugar, and flavoured with cardamom and saffron. Finished with ghee-fried cashews and raisins. The result is a creamy, fragrant, slightly thick dessert soup served warm or at room temperature. Payasam differs from North Indian kheer in using coconut milk and wheat vermicelli rather than pure cow's milk and rice. A traditional Onam Sadya and festive offering in South India and Singapore's Tamil community. Per 100g: 195.0 kcal, 4.2g protein, 8.5g fat, 28.5g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 55.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 26.5. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 83mg per bowl (150g) — very low. Payasam is naturally very low in sodium. Suitable for hypertensive patients from a sodium standpoint. The moderate sugar and fat content are the primary dietary considerations. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Moderate GI (~62); glycaemic load per bowl (150g) is approximately 27. content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Mango Lassi**. Mango Lassi is India's most popular yogurt beverage — a smooth, thick, intensely fruity drink made by blending whole-milk yogurt with ripe mango pulp (traditionally Alphonso or Kesar mango), sugar, and sometimes a pinch of cardamom. Chilled and served immediately. The mango provides a bright, tropical sweetness and the yogurt adds a creamy body and mild tang. Rich in vitamin C, beta-carotene, and calcium. Available at virtually every Indian restaurant in Singapore as a standard beverages menu item. The ratio of mango to yogurt affects both texture and sweetness. Per 100g: 88.0 kcal, 2.8g protein, 2.2g fat, 15.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 42.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 24.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from yogurt and mango pulp combination; yogurt protein and fat moderate the GI of the mango sugars. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 130mg per glass (310g) — very low. High potassium (428mg per glass) from mango and yogurt is beneficial for blood pressure. Suitable for hypertensive patients — very low sodium, high potassium from mango. The calcium from yogurt is also beneficial for blood pressure regulation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~52); glycaemic load per glass (310g) is approximately 25. The yogurt moderates the GI of mango, but the total sugar content per glass (~44g) is high. Diabetic patients should limit to half a glass (150ml) and avoid added sugar. Request sugar-free mango lassi or use less sweet mango. The yogurt protein moderates the glycaemic response significantly. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Mango is one of the richest fruit sources of vitamin C (15.5mg/100g) and beta-carotene/vitamin A (88mcg RAE/100g); yogurt provides probiotics and calcium. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the vitamin C and beta-carotene from mango support collagen synthesis and have anti-inflammatory antioxidant effects. The probiotics from yogurt support gut-immune axis health. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 25mg per glass (310g). Vitamin A is notably high from mango beta-carotene (88mcg RAE/100g, approximately 273mcg RAE per glass). The sugar content varies significantly based on mango sweetness and added sugar — values assume standard restaurant preparation with added sugar.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Sweet Lassi**. Sweet Lassi is the original and simplest form of lassi — whole-milk yogurt blended with water, sugar, and a pinch of cardamom, churned until frothy and served chilled. Originally from Punjab, it is consumed across India and in every Indian restaurant in Singapore. Cooling, refreshing, and mildly sweet with a clean yogurt tang. Very low in sodium and an excellent source of calcium and vitamin B12. The base form from which all other lassi variants (mango lassi, rose lassi, salted lassi) derive. Probiotics from fresh yogurt support digestive health. Per 100g: 72.0 kcal, 3.0g protein, 1.5g fat, 12.5g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 45.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 48 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 18.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — sweetened yogurt drink GI approximately 48; the yogurt protein and fat moderate the GI of the added sugar. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 140mg per glass (310g) — very low. Excellent source of calcium (335mg per glass) which supports blood pressure regulation. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — very low sodium and high calcium. A glass of sweet lassi provides about 30% of the daily recommended calcium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~48); glycaemic load per glass (310g) is approximately 18. The added sugar (11.8g/100g) provides 37g of sugar per glass. Diabetic patients should request sugar-free or reduced-sugar sweet lassi. The yogurt protein significantly moderates the GI of the sugar. Alternatively, salted lassi (no sugar) is a diabetes-friendly option. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Yogurt probiotics support gut microbiome health linked to immune modulation; calcium and phosphorus support bone health; cardamom has anti-inflammatory properties. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the probiotic content of fresh yogurt and the calcium support immune regulation and bone health. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 19mg per glass (310g). Vitamin B12 is well-represented from dairy. The sugar content per glass (approximately 37g) from added sugar is significant — reduced-sugar or unsweetened versions are preferable for health-conscious consumers.

Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Sweet Lassi**. Sweet Lassi is the original and simplest form of lassi — whole-milk yogurt blended with water, sugar, and a pinch of cardamom, churned until frothy and served chilled. Originally from Punjab, it is consumed across India and in every Indian restaurant in Singapore. Cooling, refreshing, and mildly sweet with a clean yogurt tang. Very low in sodium and an excellent source of calcium and vitamin B12. The base form from which all other lassi variants (mango lassi, rose lassi, salted lassi) derive. Probiotics from fresh yogurt support digestive health. Per 100g: 72.0 kcal, 3.0g protein, 1.5g fat, 12.5g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 45.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 48 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 18.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — sweetened yogurt drink GI approximately 48; the yogurt protein and fat moderate the GI of the added sugar. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 140mg per glass (310g) — very low. Excellent source of calcium (335mg per glass) which supports blood pressure regulation. Excellent choice for hypertensive patients — very low sodium and high calcium. A glass of sweet lassi provides about 30% of the daily recommended calcium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~48); glycaemic load per glass (310g) is approximately 18. The added sugar (11.8g/100g) provides 37g of sugar per glass. Diabetic patients should request sugar-free or reduced-sugar sweet lassi. The yogurt protein significantly moderates the GI of the sugar. Alternatively, salted lassi (no sugar) is a diabetes-friendly option. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Yogurt probiotics support gut microbiome health linked to immune modulation; calcium and phosphorus support bone health; cardamom has anti-inflammatory properties. Suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the probiotic content of fresh yogurt and the calcium support immune regulation and bone health. Note: Contains dairy (yogurt). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 19mg per glass (310g). Vitamin B12 is well-represented from dairy. The sugar content per glass (approximately 37g) from added sugar is significant — reduced-sugar or unsweetened versions are preferable for health-conscious consumers.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Masala Chai**. Masala Chai is India's national warming beverage — black tea brewed directly with whole aromatic spices (fresh ginger, green cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper) in water, then full-fat milk and sugar added and the whole mixture simmered together for 2–3 minutes before straining. The result is a rich, amber-coloured, milky, intensely fragrant tea with a warming, spicy kick. The spice blend varies by region and household — ginger and cardamom are universal, while cinnamon, cloves, and pepper are common additions. Available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and teh tarik counters across Singapore. Provides notable anti-inflammatory spice compounds. Per 100g: 62.0 kcal, 1.8g protein, 1.6g fat, 9.8g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 32.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 42 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 8.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from sweetened milk tea; the milk protein moderates the GI of the added sugar; spices have negligible glycaemic effect. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 67mg per cup (210g) — very low. Masala chai is one of the lowest-sodium warm beverages. The calcium and potassium from milk support blood pressure. Suitable for hypertensive patients — very low sodium. Limit added sugar. Ginger in masala chai may have modest vasodilatory effects benefiting blood pressure. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Medium. Moderate GI (~42); glycaemic load per cup (210g) is approximately 9. The sugar content (8.8g/100g) provides 18.5g of sugar per cup. Diabetic patients should request masala chai with less sugar or without sugar (the spice flavour is pleasant without sugar). The cinnamon in masala chai may improve insulin sensitivity. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and black pepper all have documented anti-inflammatory properties; the combination is one of the most potent anti-inflammatory spice blends in culinary use. Masala chai is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — the ginger, cinnamon, and cloves provide potent anti-inflammatory compounds. Ginger's gingerols and cinnamon's cinnamaldehyde have documented benefits for joint inflammation. Note: Contains dairy (milk). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 12mg per cup (210g). Caffeine content approximately 30–50mg per cup from black tea. Values assume 1.5 teaspoons sugar per cup (standard Indian chai sweetness). The anti-inflammatory spice compounds (gingerols, cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, piperine) are bioactive but contribute negligible macronutrients at standard brewing concentrations.

This is **Raita**. Raita is the essential cooling Indian condiment — plain whole-milk yogurt whisked smooth with diced fresh cucumber, a generous dusting of roasted cumin powder, a pinch of chilli powder, chaat masala, salt, and fresh coriander, all served cold. image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Basmati Rice (raw, uncooked)**. Basmati rice is a long-grain aromatic white rice originating from the Punjab and Haryana regions of India and Pakistan. The name means 'fragrant' in Hindi/Sanskrit. Raw basmati grains are elongated, slender, and characteristically become even longer (not wider) upon cooking. Basmati has a distinctly lower glycaemic index (~58) compared to jasmine or ordinary white rice (~73–89) due to its higher amylose-to-amylopectin ratio. Traditionally aged for 1–2 years after harvest to develop flavour and aroma. The primary grain for biryani, pulao, and Indian rice dishes. Nutrient values per 100g raw are comparable to other milled white rice, with modest protein and negligible fat and sodium. Per 100g: 356.0 kcal, 8.4g protein, 0.5g fat, 77.6g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 4.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 58 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 40.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked basmati rice (boiled/absorption method). Raw basmati GI is not applicable. Basmati has a significantly lower GI (~58) than jasmine rice (~73) due to its higher amylose content (28–30%), which forms a stiffer, slower-digesting gel. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Basmati rice is very low in sodium (4mg/100g raw) and does not contribute to hypertension. It is a safe staple for patients managing blood pressure. Basmati rice can be freely consumed by hypertensive patients. Attention should be directed toward sauces, gravies, and curries consumed alongside the rice, which often contain the sodium load. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Cooked basmati rice has a GI of approximately 58 — meaningfully lower than jasmine rice (GI ~73) due to its higher amylose content. This makes it a preferred rice choice for glycaemic management in South Asian patients with type 2 diabetes who eat rice daily. Basmati rice is recommended over jasmine or other high-GI white rice varieties for patients with type 2 diabetes. Portion control remains essential — limit to 100–150g cooked per meal. Parboiled or brown basmati further reduces GI to ~47–50. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Basmati rice is nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis at standard serving levels. No significant pro-inflammatory or anti-inflammatory properties. Acceptable at normal portions. Moderate substitution with red rice or black rice provides additional antioxidants and magnesium that may mildly support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns. Note: Values are per 100g raw dry grain. Cooked basmati rice yields approximately 130–140 kcal/100g cooked. Basmati's high amylose content (28–30%) is its key distinguishing nutritional feature and the reason for its lower glycaemic index compared to jasmine or other sticky rice varieties.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **White Glutinous Rice (raw, uncooked)**. White glutinous rice (also called sticky rice or waxy rice) is a short-grain variety of Oryza sativa that is almost entirely composed of amylopectin starch — the branched starch polymer that makes cooked rice sticky and clingy. Unlike regular rice which contains 18–28% amylose (the straight-chain starch), glutinous rice contains less than 1% amylose. This gives it an extremely high glycaemic index (GI ~98) when cooked. Raw glutinous rice is opaque and chalky white, distinct from translucent regular white rice. Used extensively in Southeast Asian cuisines for festive dishes: ang ku kueh, ondeh ondeh, tang yuan, pulut hitam (black glutinous rice porridge), bak chang (rice dumplings), and nasi pulut. The term 'glutinous' refers to its sticky glue-like texture, not to gluten — it is completely gluten-free. Per 100g: 356.0 kcal, 6.8g protein, 0.6g fat, 79.0g carbohydrates, 0.9g dietary fibre, 5.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 98 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 69.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked white glutinous (sticky) rice. Raw glutinous rice GI is not applicable. Cooked glutinous rice has one of the highest GI values of all rice types (GI ~98) because it is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch (>99% amylopectin, <1% amylose), which is digested extremely rapidly. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Glutinous rice is virtually sodium-free and poses no direct cardiovascular risk through blood pressure pathways. Its use is generally in small quantities in desserts rather than as a meal staple. Glutinous rice itself does not contribute to hypertension. However, preparations involving glutinous rice (such as nasi lemak using glutinous rice, or glutinous rice balls in sweet broth) may have added coconut milk and sugar that should be factored into dietary planning. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Very high. Cooked white glutinous rice has an extremely high glycaemic index (~98) — higher than even jasmine rice. It is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch, which gelatinises fully on cooking and is digested almost instantaneously. Regular consumption in patients with type 2 diabetes will cause severe postprandial blood glucose spikes. Patients with type 2 diabetes should minimise consumption of glutinous rice-based dishes (tang yuan, ondeh ondeh, ang ku kueh, pulut hitam with added sugar). If consumed, limit to a small festive portion (40–50g cooked) and pair with high-fibre foods to blunt the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Glutinous rice is not a significant inflammatory driver at the small quantities typically consumed in Singapore dessert contexts. Acceptable in small festive portions. No specific rheumatoid arthritis concerns beyond its high glycaemic load contributing to general systemic inflammation with very frequent, large-portion consumption. Note: Values are per 100g raw dry glutinous rice. Completely gluten-free despite the word 'glutinous'. Contains essentially no amylose starch — GI is among the highest of any commonly consumed grain. Black glutinous rice (pulut hitam) is a different variety with a different nutrient profile — not this record.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Variation B). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **White Glutinous Rice (raw, uncooked)**. White glutinous rice (also called sticky rice or waxy rice) is a short-grain variety of Oryza sativa that is almost entirely composed of amylopectin starch — the branched starch polymer that makes cooked rice sticky and clingy. Unlike regular rice which contains 18–28% amylose (the straight-chain starch), glutinous rice contains less than 1% amylose. This gives it an extremely high glycaemic index (GI ~98) when cooked. Raw glutinous rice is opaque and chalky white, distinct from translucent regular white rice. Used extensively in Southeast Asian cuisines for festive dishes: ang ku kueh, ondeh ondeh, tang yuan, pulut hitam (black glutinous rice porridge), bak chang (rice dumplings), and nasi pulut. The term 'glutinous' refers to its sticky glue-like texture, not to gluten — it is completely gluten-free. Per 100g: 356.0 kcal, 6.8g protein, 0.6g fat, 79.0g carbohydrates, 0.9g dietary fibre, 5.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 98 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 69.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked white glutinous (sticky) rice. Raw glutinous rice GI is not applicable. Cooked glutinous rice has one of the highest GI values of all rice types (GI ~98) because it is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch (>99% amylopectin, <1% amylose), which is digested extremely rapidly. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Glutinous rice is virtually sodium-free and poses no direct cardiovascular risk through blood pressure pathways. Its use is generally in small quantities in desserts rather than as a meal staple. Glutinous rice itself does not contribute to hypertension. However, preparations involving glutinous rice (such as nasi lemak using glutinous rice, or glutinous rice balls in sweet broth) may have added coconut milk and sugar that should be factored into dietary planning. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Very high. Cooked white glutinous rice has an extremely high glycaemic index (~98) — higher than even jasmine rice. It is composed almost entirely of amylopectin starch, which gelatinises fully on cooking and is digested almost instantaneously. Regular consumption in patients with type 2 diabetes will cause severe postprandial blood glucose spikes. Patients with type 2 diabetes should minimise consumption of glutinous rice-based dishes (tang yuan, ondeh ondeh, ang ku kueh, pulut hitam with added sugar). If consumed, limit to a small festive portion (40–50g cooked) and pair with high-fibre foods to blunt the glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Glutinous rice is not a significant inflammatory driver at the small quantities typically consumed in Singapore dessert contexts. Acceptable in small festive portions. No specific rheumatoid arthritis concerns beyond its high glycaemic load contributing to general systemic inflammation with very frequent, large-portion consumption. Note: Values are per 100g raw dry glutinous rice. Completely gluten-free despite the word 'glutinous'. Contains essentially no amylose starch — GI is among the highest of any commonly consumed grain. Black glutinous rice (pulut hitam) is a different variety with a different nutrient profile — not this record.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Rolled Oats (plain, old-fashioned, not instant)**. Rolled oats (old-fashioned oats) are whole oat groats (Avena sativa) that have been steamed and mechanically flattened into flat flakes. Unlike instant oats which are more finely cut and pre-cooked, old-fashioned rolled oats retain most of the grain's intact structure and beta-glucan content, giving them a medium GI of ~57 when cooked. Raw (dry) rolled oats contain 13.2g protein, 10.1g dietary fibre, 6.9g fat, and 67.7g carbohydrate per 100g — making them one of the most nutrient-dense breakfast grains. The soluble fibre (beta-glucan) is the key bioactive: it forms a thick gel in the gut that slows digestion, lowers LDL cholesterol by 5–10%, and blunts blood glucose spikes. Widely consumed in Singapore as a breakfast cereal, often prepared with milk or fresh fruit. Per 100g: 379.0 kcal, 13.2g protein, 6.9g fat, 67.7g carbohydrates, 10.1g dietary fibre, 5.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 57 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 15.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked rolled oats porridge (made with water). Dry oats GI is not applicable. Old-fashioned rolled oats have a medium GI (~57) significantly lower than quick-cook or instant oats (~66–83) due to larger particle size requiring slower enzymatic digestion. Beta-glucan soluble fibre significantly reduces the glycaemic response. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Rolled oats are clinically recognised as a heart-healthy grain. The beta-glucan soluble fibre (3–4g per 40g serving) reduces LDL cholesterol by 5–10% with regular consumption, supporting overall cardiovascular health and indirectly benefiting blood pressure management. Oats are actively recommended for patients with hypertension and dyslipidaemia. One bowl of plain oat porridge per day is a clinically backed dietary intervention. The critical caveat is preparation — instant flavoured oats can contain 200–500mg sodium per sachet; always choose plain rolled oats. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Old-fashioned rolled oats have a medium GI (~57) and are one of the most clinically validated breakfast foods for blood glucose control. The beta-glucan fibre forms a viscous gel in the gut that slows gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption, substantially blunting postprandial glucose spikes. Rolled oats (not instant) are strongly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes as a breakfast grain. Avoid instant oats (higher GI) and flavoured oat products (high sugar). Steel-cut oats have an even lower GI (~42). Optimal preparation: plain oats cooked with unsweetened plant milk or water. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Oats are a good source of magnesium (138mg/100g dry) and selenium (28.9mcg/100g), both of which support immune function and antioxidant defences. The anti-inflammatory properties of beta-glucan and the modest omega-6 content are favourable. Rolled oats are a highly recommended breakfast grain for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Prepare with anti-inflammatory additions such as ground flaxseed and turmeric. Avoid flavoured instant oat packets. Note: Values are per 100g dry uncooked rolled oats. Oats contain avenin (a prolamin similar to gluten) and are not safe for all celiacs, particularly those with severe gluten sensitivity. Also at risk of cross-contamination with wheat. Beta-glucan content of old-fashioned oats (~4g/100g) is clinically validated for cholesterol reduction at ≥3g/day dose. Instant oats have a significantly higher GI than old-fashioned rolled oats.

This is **Wholegrain Wheat Flour / Atta (stone-ground)**. Wholegrain wheat flour (atta) is flour milled from the entire wheat grain — bran, endosperm, and germ intact. Stone-ground atta (chakki atta) preserves more of the phytonutrients and bran structure than roller-milled wholemeal flour. Atta is finely milled to a soft, slightly coarse texture ideal for rolling out unleavened flatbreads (chapati, roti, paratha). image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Rice Flour (plain, raw, from long-grain white rice)**. Rice flour is a fine white powder milled from long-grain white rice. Completely gluten-free. It is the primary flour used in many Southeast Asian and South Indian preparations: idiyappam (string hoppers), murukku, kueh (Singaporean Chinese cakes), rice paper, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli). Nutritionally similar to white rice — high in starch, low in protein and fibre compared to wheat flour. The GI when cooked is high (~78+). Distinct from glutinous rice flour (made from waxy rice) — the two are not interchangeable in recipes as they produce different textures. Per 100g: 366.0 kcal, 5.9g protein, 1.4g fat, 80.1g carbohydrates, 2.4g dietary fibre, 0.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 78 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 18.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of rice flour-based preparations (rice porridge, congee ~78). Dry rice flour GI not applicable. Rice flour has a high GI when cooked (products range 70–95 depending on preparation). Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Rice flour contains negligible sodium (0mg/100g) and has no direct effect on blood pressure. Its nutritional profile is similar to white rice with slightly higher starch content. Rice flour-based preparations (kueh, idiyappam) are suitable for hypertensive patients. Blood pressure concerns arise from added ingredients (coconut milk, salt, sauces) rather than the flour itself. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Rice flour has a very high GI when cooked (~78+). Products made from rice flour (idiyappam, kueh, rice noodles) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation. Lower fibre than wholegrain alternatives. Patients with type 2 diabetes should moderate consumption of rice flour-based foods. When used in cooking, combining with protein and fat helps blunt the glycaemic response. For baking and kueh-making, partial substitution with almond flour or chickpea flour reduces the GI. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Rice flour is gluten-free, making it a safe grain alternative for the subset of rheumatoid arthritis patients who are also gluten-sensitive. It provides only modest anti-inflammatory benefit. Rice flour is an acceptable gluten-free flour choice. For patients with rheumatoid arthritis seeking more anti-inflammatory grain options, almond or chickpea flour provides additional micronutrients. Note: Values are per 100g dry rice flour. Distinct from glutinous (waxy) rice flour — these are not interchangeable. Rice flour has no gluten and produces non-elastic doughs. High starch content gives baked/steamed products a chewy or crumbly texture depending on the water ratio.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chickpea Flour / Besan / Gram Flour (raw)**. Chickpea flour (besan/gram flour) is a fine, pale yellow flour milled from split Bengal gram (chana dal, a variety of chickpea with smaller, darker seeds). It has a slightly earthy, nutty flavour and is completely gluten-free. With 22.4g protein and 10.8g dietary fibre per 100g, it is far more nutritious than rice or refined wheat flour. The GI when cooked is very low (~39), making it the preferred flour for diabetic-friendly Indian cooking. Extraordinarily high in folate (437mcg DFE/100g) — essential for pregnant women and those with folate deficiency. Used extensively in Indian cooking: pakora batter, dhokla, besan halwa, chana dal flour flatbreads, murukku, and as a natural gluten-free thickener for gravies. Per 100g: 387.0 kcal, 22.4g protein, 6.7g fat, 57.8g carbohydrates, 10.8g dietary fibre, 64.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 39 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 6.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked besan-based dishes. Dry besan GI is not applicable. Chickpea flour has a low GI (~39) due to its resistant starch and high protein and fibre content, which substantially slow carbohydrate digestion. This makes it one of the lowest-GI flours available. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Chickpea flour is rich in potassium (846mg/100g) and magnesium (166mg/100g) — minerals clinically associated with blood pressure reduction. Sodium content is modest (64mg/100g). Besan is a heart-healthy flour choice for patients with hypertension. Replacing refined wheat flour with chickpea flour in Indian preparations (pakora batter, flatbreads) reduces glycaemic load and increases potassium and magnesium intake. Note that deep-frying with besan (pakoras) adds significant fat. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Chickpea flour has one of the lowest GI values of any flour (~39). High in protein (22.4g/100g) and dietary fibre (10.8g/100g). It is an exceptional flour choice for patients with type 2 diabetes as it substantially reduces postprandial glucose spikes compared to wheat or rice flours. Strongly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes — substitute besan for maida in pancakes, batter, flatbreads. Besan cheela (chickpea flour pancakes) are a clinically recommended high-protein, low-GI breakfast option for diabetic patients. Extremely high folate content (437mcg DFE) is a nutritional bonus. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high in folate (437mcg DFE/100g), iron (6.2mg), and magnesium (166mg/100g). Folate supports immune cell regulation. Low in anti-nutritional factors when cooked. Gluten-free. Chickpea flour is an excellent high-nutrient, gluten-free alternative to wheat flour for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Its anti-inflammatory legume proteins and high folate make it a recommended flour. Some RA patients have concurrent gluten sensitivity; besan is safe for them. Note: Values per 100g dry flour. Besan is made from desi chickpeas (Cicer arietinum var. desi) which are smaller and darker than kabuli chickpeas. Distinct from standard chickpea flour made from kabuli chickpeas in Western markets — desi besan has slightly higher protein and fibre. Raw besan has a raw legume flavour; dry-roasting improves taste significantly.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Semolina / Rava / Sooji (raw, uncooked, from durum wheat)**. Semolina (rava/sooji) is the coarsely milled endosperm of durum wheat. Pale cream-yellow in colour with a slightly coarse, sandy texture. In Indian cooking, it is the primary ingredient for upma (savoury semolina porridge), rava dosa (thin crispy crepes), rava idli, and kesari/halwa (sweet semolina pudding). In Mediterranean cooking, it is the base for pasta, couscous, and Arabic sweets. Dry-roasting rava in a pan before adding water is standard in South Indian cooking — it deepens the flavour and prevents clumping. Nutritionally, semolina has 12.7g protein, 3.9g fibre, and a moderate GI (~65) when cooked, making it a better white starch choice than plain white rice but inferior to whole grains. Per 100g: 360.0 kcal, 12.7g protein, 1.1g fat, 72.8g carbohydrates, 3.9g dietary fibre, 1.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 23.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked semolina porridge and upma (approximately 65). Dry semolina GI is not applicable. Semolina has a moderate GI (~65) because durum wheat starch gelatinises at a slower rate than soft wheat starch due to larger particle size and protein matrix. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Semolina is virtually sodium-free (1mg/100g) and does not contribute to blood pressure. It provides potassium (186mg/100g) and magnesium (47mg/100g) which are mildly beneficial for vascular function. Plain semolina preparations (upma, rava dosa) have low sodium. The clinical concern is with added salt during cooking — standard upma recipes add 1–2g sodium per serving from salt, mustard seeds in oil, and added vegetables. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Cooked semolina-based dishes (upma, rava dosa) have a moderate GI (~65). Higher than wholegrain alternatives but lower than refined white rice or white bread. The protein matrix of durum wheat moderates the glycaemic response compared to maida. Semolina upma is an acceptable breakfast option for patients with type 2 diabetes at moderate portions (100–150g cooked). Adding vegetables and using minimal oil further reduces the effective glycaemic impact. Rava dosa is less favourable than vegetable upma due to higher oil content. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Semolina provides selenium (47.3mcg/100g) and modest magnesium. Contains gluten. Acceptable for most patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Contains gluten — avoid if concurrent coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity is present. Wholegrain atta is a superior anti-inflammatory grain choice. Note: Values per 100g dry semolina. Contains gluten — not safe for coeliac disease. Distinct from fine semolina (pasta semolina) and from coarse semolina used in some Mediterranean recipes. Indian rava/sooji is typically medium-coarse to coarse. Fine rava and coarse rava have slightly different textures but nearly identical nutritional profiles.

Nutritionally, semolina has 12.7g protein, 3.9g fibre, and a moderate GI (~65) when cooked, making it a better white starch choice than plain white rice but inferior to whole grains. Per 100g: 360.0 kcal, 12.7g protein, 1.1g fat, 72.8g carbohydrates, 3.9g dietary fibre, 1.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 23.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked semolina porridge and upma (approximately 65). Dry semolina GI is not applicable. Semolina has a moderate GI (~65) because durum wheat starch gelatinises at a slower rate than soft wheat starch due to larger particle size and protein matrix. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Semolina is virtually sodium-free (1mg/100g) and does not contribute to blood pressure. It provides potassium (186mg/100g) and magnesium (47mg/100g) which are mildly beneficial for vascular function. Plain semolina preparations (upma, rava dosa) have low sodium. The clinical concern is with added salt during cooking — standard upma recipes add 1–2g sodium per serving from salt, mustard seeds in oil, and added vegetables. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Cooked semolina-based dishes (upma, rava dosa) have a moderate GI (~65). Higher than wholegrain alternatives but lower than refined white rice or white bread. The protein matrix of durum wheat moderates the glycaemic response compared to maida. Semolina upma is an acceptable breakfast option for patients with type 2 diabetes at moderate portions (100–150g cooked). Adding vegetables and using minimal oil further reduces the effective glycaemic impact. Rava dosa is less favourable than vegetable upma due to higher oil content. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Semolina provides selenium (47.3mcg/100g) and modest magnesium. Contains gluten. Acceptable for most patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Contains gluten — avoid if concurrent coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity is present. Wholegrain atta is a superior anti-inflammatory grain choice. Note: Values per 100g dry semolina. Contains gluten — not safe for coeliac disease. Distinct from fine semolina (pasta semolina) and from coarse semolina used in some Mediterranean recipes. Indian rava/sooji is typically medium-coarse to coarse. Fine rava and coarse rava have slightly different textures but nearly identical nutritional profiles.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Dried Rice Vermicelli / Bee Hoon (raw, uncooked)**. Dried rice vermicelli (bee hoon/bihun) is a thin, dried noodle made from rice flour and water, extruded through small-diameter dies. In dried form, the noodles are brittle, opaque white strands. Before cooking they must be soaked in water for 15–30 minutes until softened, then drained and stir-fried or added to soups. Made from pure rice flour — completely gluten-free. A fundamental noodle in Singapore and SEA hawker cooking: mee siam, bee hoon goreng, Teochew fish soup bee hoon, laksa, and thin rice noodle salads. Nutritionally similar to white rice — high in starch with modest protein and minimal fibre. The GI when cooked (~61) is slightly lower than jasmine rice. Per 100g: 364.0 kcal, 6.0g protein, 0.6g fat, 80.1g carbohydrates, 1.9g dietary fibre, 34.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 61 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 44.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of cooked rice vermicelli (~61). Dry rice vermicelli GI is not applicable. Cooked rice vermicelli has a moderate GI (~61), somewhat lower than jasmine rice due to the processing involved in noodle extrusion which partially modifies starch gelatinisation behaviour. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Dried bee hoon contains modest sodium (34mg/100g dry) and is not a major contributor to blood pressure. The sodium load in bee hoon dishes comes from cooking sauces (fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce). Plain rice vermicelli is low-sodium. Patients with hypertension should limit the added sauces in bee hoon dishes and request reduced-sauce preparations at hawker stalls. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Cooked rice vermicelli has a moderate GI (~61). At a typical 75g dry / 180g cooked serving, the glycaemic load is substantial. Lower GI than jasmine rice but higher than most wholegrain options. Rice vermicelli dishes at hawker stalls are acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions. Request to increase the vegetable and protein components (beansprouts, egg, tofu, prawns) relative to the noodle portion. Avoid sweet sauces added to mee siam. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Gluten-free. Nutritionally modest — low in anti-inflammatory micronutrients. Neutral for rheumatoid arthritis. Acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Safe for those with concurrent gluten sensitivity. Does not provide significant anti-inflammatory benefit on its own; focus on the protein and vegetable components of the dish. Note: Values per 100g dry uncooked bee hoon. Gluten-free but may be manufactured in facilities with wheat products. Cooked bee hoon weighs approximately 2.4x the dry weight after soaking and cooking. Distinct from glass noodles (cellophane noodles made from mung bean starch), which have a different texture and slightly different nutritional profile.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Fresh Yellow Noodle / Hokkien Mee Noodle (raw, uncooked)**. Fresh yellow noodles (Hokkien mee noodles) are a refrigerated fresh wheat noodle made from wheat flour mixed with lye water (an alkaline solution of sodium and potassium carbonates) and sometimes egg. The alkaline treatment gives the noodles their characteristic yellow colour, springy texture, and slightly alkaline smell. Sold in vacuum-sealed portions at Singapore supermarkets and wet markets. Medium thickness, round cross-section. The primary noodle in Singapore hokkien hae mee (prawn noodles), Hokkien char mee, char kway teow (sometimes mixed), and mee goreng. Higher in sodium than dried bee hoon due to lye water content. Lower in calories per 100g than dried bee hoon because it contains ~63% water. Per 100g: 138.0 kcal, 4.7g protein, 0.6g fat, 28.8g carbohydrates, 1.2g dietary fibre, 212.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 57 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 24.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of fresh cooked wheat noodles (approximately 57). Fresh yellow noodles have a lower GI than white bread due to the dense gluten matrix slowing starch gelatinisation. Alkaline treatment (lye water) may slightly modify starch digestion rate. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Fresh yellow noodles contain significant sodium from lye water processing (approximately 212mg/100g raw). A 150g hawker serving contains ~318mg sodium from the noodle alone — before any sauces, oyster sauce, soy sauce, or other seasonings are added which typically add 600–1200mg sodium more. For patients with hypertension, fresh yellow noodle dishes at hawker stalls are high-sodium meals. Request reduced sauce, or choose soup-based noodle dishes where sauce can be adjusted. A typical hokkien mee or char kway teow dish can contain 1500–2000mg sodium total. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Cooked fresh wheat noodles have a moderate GI (~57). The glycaemic load per standard 150g hawker serving is substantial (~24.8). Lower GI than white rice but the stir-frying with oil and sugary sauces in hawker preparations increases overall energy density. Patients with type 2 diabetes can consume hawker noodle dishes in moderation. Request extra vegetables and reduced sauce. Avoid sweet preparations (mee goreng with sweet soy, laksa with additional sugar). Soup noodles (mee soup, fish ball noodles) are lower in fat and sodium than dry/stir-fried preparations. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Fresh yellow noodles contain gluten and are nutritionally modest in anti-inflammatory factors. Neutral for rheumatoid arthritis at standard portions. Acceptable at normal hawker portions. Contains gluten — contraindicated if concurrent coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity. For patients with rheumatoid arthritis, focus on increasing the vegetable and lean protein components of noodle dishes. Note: Values per 100g raw fresh uncooked noodle. Fresh yellow noodles are typically not halal due to possible use of non-halal egg or production processes; many hawker stalls use halal-certified versions. The characteristic yellow colour and alkaline pH come from lye water (sodium/potassium carbonate solution). Shelf life is short (2–3 days refrigerated). Distinct from flat white kway teow (rice flour noodles) which are coarser and gluten-free.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Yellow Moong Dal / Split Mung Bean (raw, dried)**. Yellow moong dal (split mung bean) is the husked, split form of whole green mung beans (Vigna radiata). The splitting and dehusking exposes the pale yellow interior, resulting in a soft, quick-cooking dal that requires no soaking. The highest fibre content of the common Indian dals (16.3g/100g dry). Nutritionally exceptional — very high in potassium (1246mg), folate (625mcg DFE), and magnesium (189mg) per 100g. Considered the most easily digestible pulse in South Asian culinary tradition. Used for simple dal dishes, khichdi, pongal (with rice), payasam (sweet dessert), and as the base for cheela (lentil pancakes). A recommended food for infants, elderly, and patients with gastrointestinal conditions. Per 100g: 348.0 kcal, 24.5g protein, 1.2g fat, 58.6g carbohydrates, 16.3g dietary fibre, 15.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 31 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 9.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled split moong dal (approximately 31). Dry moong dal GI not applicable. Very low GI due to very high fibre content and intact legume cell matrix slowing starch digestion. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Extraordinarily high in potassium (1246mg/100g dry) and magnesium (189mg/100g) — among the highest of any commonly consumed food. Very low sodium (15mg/100g). Exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio for blood pressure management. Yellow moong dal is one of the most cardiovascular-protective legumes. Daily consumption is recommended for patients with hypertension. Moong dal soup or khichdi (moong dal + rice) prepared without excess salt is clinically ideal for blood pressure management. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low GI (~31) when boiled. Extremely high in dietary fibre (16.3g/100g dry) and protein (24.5g). The highest fibre content of the common dals, making it exceptionally effective at blunting postprandial blood glucose spikes. Yellow moong dal is particularly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes because it is highly digestible (husked and split) while maintaining a very low GI. Moong dal khichdi is a classic Indian therapeutic food for diabetic patients — combining moong dal with parboiled or brown rice further improves the glycaemic profile. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Exceptional folate content (625mcg DFE/100g dry) — the highest of commonly consumed Indian dals. High in calcium (132mg/100g), magnesium (189mg/100g), and iron (6.7mg). Moong dal is traditionally described as the most easily digestible dal in Ayurvedic medicine. Yellow moong dal is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. It is digestible, anti-inflammatory through its folate and magnesium content, and provides plant-based protein to support muscle maintenance. Ideal for flare periods when gut function is compromised. Note: Values per 100g dry split husked moong dal. Does not require soaking — cooks in 20–30 minutes. The extremely high folate content (625mcg DFE) is especially notable — 50g serving provides over 300mcg DFE, exceeding the daily recommended intake for most adults. Distinct from whole green mung beans (which have a different nutritional profile with intact skin).
