image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Toor Dal / Split Pigeon Peas (raw, dried)**. Toor dal (arhar dal) is split pigeon peas (Cajanus cajan) — the most widely consumed dal in South and Western India and the foundational ingredient for sambar. Pale yellow, slightly larger than moong dal. Requires longer cooking than moong dal — pressure cooking for 15–20 minutes is standard. Has a slightly earthy, robust flavour that holds up to the tamarind, tomato, and spice-heavy sambar preparation. Extremely high in potassium (1392mg/100g dry), making it exceptional for blood pressure management. Very high in folate (456mcg DFE) and magnesium (183mg). GI is very low (~32) when cooked. Nutritionally comparable to masoor and moong dal but with a distinct flavour profile preferred in Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, and Gujarat cuisines. Per 100g: 335.0 kcal, 22.3g protein, 1.7g fat, 57.6g carbohydrates, 15.0g dietary fibre, 17.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 32 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 9.2. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled toor/pigeon peas (approximately 32). Dry toor dal GI not applicable. Very low GI characteristic of legumes, due to resistant starch, high protein, and dietary fibre content. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely high in potassium (1392mg/100g dry) — the highest of the common South Indian dals. Very high magnesium (183mg) and very low sodium (17mg). This exceptional mineral profile directly supports blood pressure regulation. Toor dal (sambar base) is a clinically excellent food for patients with hypertension. Sambar made with toor dal, tomato, and vegetables provides a high-potassium, low-sodium meal component. Limit added salt during preparation. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low GI (~32) when boiled. High dietary fibre (15g/100g dry) and protein (22.3g). South Indian sambar, the primary preparation of toor dal, is a clinically beneficial dish for diabetic patients when served with brown rice or ragi idli. Toor dal is strongly recommended as a daily protein source for patients with type 2 diabetes. The combination of toor dal sambar with par-boiled rice is a significantly better glycaemic combination than white rice alone. Tamarind in sambar may also have modest glycaemic benefits. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. High folate (456mcg DFE/100g dry), potassium, and magnesium. Anti-inflammatory plant proteins. Toor dal is a traditional South Indian health food. Toor dal is recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a daily vegetarian protein. The traditional South Indian use of anti-inflammatory spices in sambar (turmeric, cumin, coriander, asafoetida) enhances the anti-inflammatory value of toor dal dishes. Note: Values per 100g dry split toor dal. Soak for 30–60 minutes before cooking or pressure cook without soaking. The oily variety of toor dal (tel wali arhar) is coated with castor oil for preservation and should be washed thoroughly before use — nutritional values are for unwashed dry grain without coating.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Urad Dal / Black Gram Dal (split, husked, raw, dried)**. Urad dal is split, dehusked black gram (Vigna mungo). The outer black hull is removed during processing to reveal the cream-white interior. Unlike other dals which cook into a smooth porridge, urad dal has a distinctive mucilaginous (slimy) quality when soaked and ground — this property is essential for the sticky, fermentable batter used to make idli and dosa. The fermentation of urad dal batter with soaked rice (overnight at room temperature) is one of the most important food processes in South Indian cuisine. Nutritionally outstanding: highest dietary fibre (18.3g/100g), highest magnesium (267mg/100g), and high protein (26.1g/100g) of the common dals. Also used for medu vada (fried lentil doughnuts), papadum, and tadka in dal preparations. Per 100g: 341.0 kcal, 26.1g protein, 1.8g fat, 57.6g carbohydrates, 18.3g dietary fibre, 38.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 43 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 12.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled black gram / urad dal (approximately 43). Dry urad dal GI not applicable. The higher GI compared to red lentils and moong dal reflects the different starch-protein matrix and fermentation effects in idli/dosa preparations. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high in magnesium (267mg/100g dry) — the highest magnesium content of the common dals. Very high in potassium (983mg) and calcium (138mg). Magnesium is directly associated with arterial relaxation and blood pressure reduction. Urad dal is recommended for patients with hypertension. Its magnesium content is clinically significant — 50g dry urad dal provides 134mg magnesium, a substantial fraction of the 350–400mg/day adult recommended intake. Idli made with urad dal batter is a low-sodium, high-magnesium breakfast option. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Moderate GI (~43) when boiled. Very high in dietary fibre (18.3g/100g dry) and protein (26.1g). In idli/dosa form (after fermentation), the GI of idli (~77) is driven more by the rice component than the urad dal. Urad dal as a dal side dish has a moderate GI (~43) — acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes. The fermentation process in idli/dosa batter increases B vitamin bioavailability. Ragi (finger millet) idli using urad dal batter is recommended as a lower-GI variation for diabetic patients. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Exceptional magnesium content (267mg/100g) — highest of common dals. Very high iron (7.6mg/100g), zinc (3.4mg), calcium (138mg), and phosphorus (379mg). These minerals support bone health and immune function relevant to RA management. Urad dal is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The very high magnesium, iron, and zinc content makes it one of the most micronutrient-dense plant proteins. Anti-inflammatory properties of fermented preparations (idli, dosa) are additionally beneficial. Note: Values per 100g dry split husked urad dal. The mucilaginous properties of soaked urad dal are caused by a specific mucilage polysaccharide that aids in fermentation. Soaking urad dal for idli batter: typically 4–6 hours. Fermentation time 8–12 hours at room temperature. Distinct from whole urad (whole black gram) which retains the black outer hull.

Exceptional magnesium content (267mg/100g) — highest of common dals. Very high iron (7.6mg/100g), zinc (3.4mg), calcium (138mg), and phosphorus (379mg). These minerals support bone health and immune function relevant to RA management. Urad dal is highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The very high magnesium, iron, and zinc content makes it one of the most micronutrient-dense plant proteins. Anti-inflammatory properties of fermented preparations (idli, dosa) are additionally beneficial. Note: Values per 100g dry split husked urad dal. The mucilaginous properties of soaked urad dal are caused by a specific mucilage polysaccharide that aids in fermentation. Soaking urad dal for idli batter: typically 4–6 hours. Fermentation time 8–12 hours at room temperature. Distinct from whole urad (whole black gram) which retains the black outer hull.

This is **Chickpeas / Kabuli Chana (raw, dried, whole)**. Chickpeas (kabuli chana) are large, cream-coloured whole legumes (Cicer arietinum, kabuli variety). They require overnight soaking (8–12 hours) followed by 45–60 minutes of boiling or 20 minutes of pressure cooking. One of the world's most widely consumed legumes. In Indian cooking: chana masala, chole bhature, sundal, and hummus. Very high in dietary fibre (17.4g/100g dry), protein (20.5g), folate (557mcg DFE), and iron (6.2mg). Exceptionally low GI (~28). Distinct from smaller desi chickpeas (chana dal) which are used to make besan flour. The aquafaba (cooking liquid) from chickpeas is a vegan egg substitute. Per 100g: 378.0 kcal, 20.5g protein, 6.0g fat, 62.9g carbohydrates, 17.4g dietary fibre, 24.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 28 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 8.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled chickpeas (approximately 28). Dry chickpea GI not applicable. Very low GI characteristic of legumes with thick seed coat. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high in potassium (875mg/100g dry) and magnesium (115mg/100g). Very low sodium. Among the highest calcium content of common legumes (105mg/100g). Chickpeas are an excellent food for patients with hypertension. Regular consumption as part of a legume-rich diet is associated with reduced systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low GI (~28) when boiled. Very high dietary fibre (17.4g/100g dry). Evidence-backed food for blood glucose control. The combination of resistant starch, protein, and soluble fibre creates a strong second-meal effect — moderating blood glucose at the next meal as well. Chickpeas are strongly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes. Chana masala, hummus, roasted chickpeas as a snack, and chickpea salads are all excellent choices. Canned chickpeas (drained and rinsed) are a convenient alternative with similar GI. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high folate (557mcg DFE/100g dry) and zinc (3.4mg/100g). Anti-inflammatory properties. Good source of manganese which is important for cartilage formation. Chickpeas are highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a protein-rich legume. The folate content is especially important for patients on methotrexate. Regular legume consumption is associated with reduced inflammatory markers. Note: Values per 100g dry whole chickpeas. Must be soaked 8–12 hours before cooking. Raw dried chickpeas must never be consumed uncooked — they contain lectins and other anti-nutritional factors that cause digestive distress. Canned chickpeas (drained) have approximately 40% lower sodium than canned with brine and are nutritionally comparable to home-cooked.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Red Kidney Beans / Rajma (raw, dried, whole)**. Red kidney beans (rajma) are large, deep red-coloured dried legumes (Phaseolus vulgaris). Require overnight soaking (8–12 hours) and must be boiled vigorously for at least 10 minutes to destroy naturally occurring phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) lectins — slow cooking in a slow cooker at low temperatures is not sufficient for lectin destruction and can cause serious food poisoning. After safe preparation, they have one of the lowest GI values of any food (~24) and the highest dietary fibre content of common legumes (24.9g/100g dry). Used in North Indian rajma curry, Mexican chilli con carne, red bean soup, and rice and peas (Caribbean). The Kashmiri red kidney bean (small variety) is particularly prized in North Indian cuisine. Per 100g: 337.0 kcal, 22.5g protein, 1.1g fat, 61.3g carbohydrates, 24.9g dietary fibre, 12.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 24 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 7.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled red kidney beans (approximately 24). Dry kidney bean GI not applicable. Extremely low GI — one of the lowest of all legumes due to very thick seed coat and high resistant starch content. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Highest potassium content of common legumes (1406mg/100g dry). Very high magnesium (138mg) and calcium (143mg). Exceptional cardiovascular mineral profile with minimal sodium (12mg/100g). Red kidney beans are an outstanding food choice for patients with hypertension. One standard serving of rajma curry (50g dry beans cooked) provides ~703mg potassium and ~69mg magnesium. Include rajma as a weekly protein source in the dietary plan for hypertensive patients. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely low GI (~24) — among the lowest of all foods. The highest dietary fibre content of common legumes (24.9g/100g dry). The resistant starch in kidney beans is exceptionally slowly digested and has a substantial second-meal effect. Kidney beans are one of the most evidence-backed foods for type 2 diabetes management. Rajma chawal (kidney beans with rice) is a superior meal combination compared to plain rice — the kidney beans dramatically lower the overall meal glycaemic index. Patients should aim to include legumes including kidney beans at least 4 times per week. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high in iron (8.2mg/100g dry) — highest of common legumes. High folate (394mcg DFE), potassium, and magnesium. Anti-inflammatory plant proteins. Red kidney beans are recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Their high iron content is especially valuable for female patients with RA who are at risk of iron deficiency anaemia. The folate content complements methotrexate therapy by replenishing folate stores. Note: CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY NOTE: Raw red kidney beans contain high levels of phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) lectin. Must be soaked overnight and boiled vigorously (rolling boil) for at least 10 minutes before consumption. Undercooking (e.g., in a slow cooker) can actually increase PHA toxicity. Symptoms of PHA poisoning: severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea within 1–3 hours. Values are per 100g dry raw beans before cooking.

This is **Red Kidney Beans / Rajma (raw, dried, whole)**. Red kidney beans (rajma) are large, deep red-coloured dried legumes (Phaseolus vulgaris). Require overnight soaking (8–12 hours) and must be boiled vigorously for at least 10 minutes to destroy naturally occurring phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) lectins — slow cooking in a slow cooker at low temperatures is not sufficient for lectin destruction and can cause serious food poisoning. After safe preparation, they have one of the lowest GI values of any food (~24) and the highest dietary fibre content of common legumes (24.9g/100g dry). Used in North Indian rajma curry, Mexican chilli con carne, red bean soup, and rice and peas (Caribbean). The Kashmiri red kidney bean (small variety) is particularly prized in North Indian cuisine. Per 100g: 337.0 kcal, 22.5g protein, 1.1g fat, 61.3g carbohydrates, 24.9g dietary fibre, 12.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 24 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 7.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled red kidney beans (approximately 24). Dry kidney bean GI not applicable. Extremely low GI — one of the lowest of all legumes due to very thick seed coat and high resistant starch content. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Highest potassium content of common legumes (1406mg/100g dry). Very high magnesium (138mg) and calcium (143mg). Exceptional cardiovascular mineral profile with minimal sodium (12mg/100g). Red kidney beans are an outstanding food choice for patients with hypertension. One standard serving of rajma curry (50g dry beans cooked) provides ~703mg potassium and ~69mg magnesium. Include rajma as a weekly protein source in the dietary plan for hypertensive patients. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely low GI (~24) — among the lowest of all foods. The highest dietary fibre content of common legumes (24.9g/100g dry). The resistant starch in kidney beans is exceptionally slowly digested and has a substantial second-meal effect. Kidney beans are one of the most evidence-backed foods for type 2 diabetes management. Rajma chawal (kidney beans with rice) is a superior meal combination compared to plain rice — the kidney beans dramatically lower the overall meal glycaemic index. Patients should aim to include legumes including kidney beans at least 4 times per week. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Very high in iron (8.2mg/100g dry) — highest of common legumes. High folate (394mcg DFE), potassium, and magnesium. Anti-inflammatory plant proteins. Red kidney beans are recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Their high iron content is especially valuable for female patients with RA who are at risk of iron deficiency anaemia. The folate content complements methotrexate therapy by replenishing folate stores. Note: CRITICAL FOOD SAFETY NOTE: Raw red kidney beans contain high levels of phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) lectin. Must be soaked overnight and boiled vigorously (rolling boil) for at least 10 minutes before consumption. Undercooking (e.g., in a slow cooker) can actually increase PHA toxicity. Symptoms of PHA poisoning: severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea within 1–3 hours. Values are per 100g dry raw beans before cooking.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Whole Green Mung Beans (raw, dried, whole, with skin)**. Whole green mung beans are small, oval, olive-green dried legumes (Vigna radiata) with the skin intact. Distinct from split yellow moong dal (which has the hull removed). Can be prepared in several ways: boiled whole for sweet Chinese green bean soup (tang shui/tong sui), cooked savoury as dal or curry, or sprouted to make bean sprouts. Can be soaked and cooked without prolonged soaking (1–2 hours soaking sufficient). Nutritionally essentially equivalent to split moong dal with the addition of more dietary fibre from the intact skin. Extremely high in potassium (1246mg), magnesium (189mg), and folate (625mcg DFE) per 100g dry. GI when cooked ~31 — very low. Per 100g: 347.0 kcal, 23.9g protein, 1.2g fat, 62.6g carbohydrates, 16.3g dietary fibre, 15.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 31 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 9.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled whole mung beans (approximately 31). Dry mung bean GI not applicable. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely high in potassium (1246mg/100g dry) and magnesium (189mg/100g). Very low in sodium (15mg/100g). Mung beans are one of the most potassium-dense whole foods. Whole mung beans are recommended for patients with hypertension. Chinese green bean soup (kacang hijau dessert) — even with modest sugar — provides substantial potassium. Plain cooked mung bean congee or savoury mung bean curry are excellent no-sodium options. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low GI (~31) when boiled. Very high in fibre (16.3g/100g dry) and protein. The intact seed coat of whole mung beans provides additional dietary fibre compared to split moong dal. Whole mung beans are highly recommended for diabetic patients. Savoury moong bean preparations are preferred over sweet Chinese green bean soup (which adds sugar). Sprouted mung beans (bean sprouts) have a lower calorie density and can be consumed in larger portions. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Exceptionally high in folate (625mcg DFE/100g), magnesium (189mg), and iron (6.7mg). Mung beans are a foundational protein in traditional South and Southeast Asian medicine for joint health. Whole mung beans are highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. In Chinese dietary therapy, mung bean soup is considered cooling and anti-inflammatory — a recommendation that aligns with the high mineral and folate content. Note: Values per 100g dry whole mung beans. Whole mung beans have essentially the same nutritional profile as split yellow moong dal with the added benefit of more dietary fibre from the intact seed coat. Sprouting dramatically increases vitamin C content and reduces phytate content, improving mineral bioavailability.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Soya Beans / Soybeans (raw, dried, whole, yellow)**. Soybeans (Glycine max) are the foundational legume of East and Southeast Asian cuisines. Raw dried yellow soybeans are rarely consumed directly — they are almost universally processed: pressed for soy milk (and coagulated into tofu), fermented into tempeh, miso, natto, and soy sauce, or extruded into textured soy protein. Nutritionally extraordinary: 36.5g protein per 100g dry (the highest protein content of any common legume), 19.9g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated — linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids), and extremely low GI (~15). Very high in calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), potassium (1797mg), and magnesium (280mg). Contains isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) — plant oestrogens with modest cardiovascular and cancer-protective properties. The reference nutritional record for tofu, soy milk, tempeh, and all soy-based products. Per 100g: 446.0 kcal, 36.5g protein, 19.9g fat, 30.2g carbohydrates, 9.3g dietary fibre, 2.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 15 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 2.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled soybeans (approximately 15). Dry soybean GI not applicable. Soybeans have one of the lowest GI values of all legumes due to very high protein content (~37%) and high fat content (~20%), which substantially slow gastric emptying and carbohydrate digestion. The carbohydrate content is also relatively low for a legume. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Highest potassium content of any common legume (1797mg/100g dry). Very high magnesium (280mg) and calcium (277mg). Very low sodium (2mg/100g). Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) may also have modest blood pressure-reducing effects through endothelial function. Regular consumption of soy products (tofu, soy milk, tempeh) is associated with cardiovascular benefit including modest blood pressure reduction. For hypertensive patients, soy-based proteins are an excellent choice compared to salt-heavy processed foods. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely low GI (~15) when boiled — among the lowest of any food. Very high protein (36.5g/100g dry) and healthy fat (19.9g), which together dramatically slow carbohydrate absorption. Soy isoflavones may also improve insulin sensitivity. Soybeans and soy products are recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes. Plain firm tofu, unsweetened soy milk, and tempeh are excellent daily protein sources. Fermented soy products (tempeh, miso in small amounts) provide additional health benefits. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Exceptional mineral content: highest magnesium (280mg), calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), phosphorus (704mg), zinc (4.9mg) of any common legume. Soy isoflavones have weak anti-inflammatory properties. Soy products are recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a complete plant protein source. Tofu and tempeh are the most accessible forms in Singapore. Note: some patients with thyroid conditions should moderate soy intake due to possible interference with thyroid hormone absorption when consumed in very large amounts. Note: Values per 100g dry raw soybeans. CRITICAL: Raw soybeans contain trypsin inhibitors and haemagglutinins that must be deactivated by thorough cooking (boiling 3–4 hours or pressure cooking 45–60 minutes). Soybeans are one of the 14 major allergens regulated in Singapore. Contains complete protein (all essential amino acids) — the only plant food to do so at comparable quality to animal proteins.

Soybeans are one of the 14 major allergens regulated in Singapore. Contains complete protein (all essential amino acids) — the only plant food to do so at comparable quality to animal proteins.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Variation B). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Soya Beans / Soybeans (raw, dried, whole, yellow)**. Soybeans (Glycine max) are the foundational legume of East and Southeast Asian cuisines. Raw dried yellow soybeans are rarely consumed directly — they are almost universally processed: pressed for soy milk (and coagulated into tofu), fermented into tempeh, miso, natto, and soy sauce, or extruded into textured soy protein. Nutritionally extraordinary: 36.5g protein per 100g dry (the highest protein content of any common legume), 19.9g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated — linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids), and extremely low GI (~15). Very high in calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), potassium (1797mg), and magnesium (280mg). Contains isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) — plant oestrogens with modest cardiovascular and cancer-protective properties. The reference nutritional record for tofu, soy milk, tempeh, and all soy-based products. Per 100g: 446.0 kcal, 36.5g protein, 19.9g fat, 30.2g carbohydrates, 9.3g dietary fibre, 2.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 15 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 2.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of boiled soybeans (approximately 15). Dry soybean GI not applicable. Soybeans have one of the lowest GI values of all legumes due to very high protein content (~37%) and high fat content (~20%), which substantially slow gastric emptying and carbohydrate digestion. The carbohydrate content is also relatively low for a legume. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Highest potassium content of any common legume (1797mg/100g dry). Very high magnesium (280mg) and calcium (277mg). Very low sodium (2mg/100g). Soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) may also have modest blood pressure-reducing effects through endothelial function. Regular consumption of soy products (tofu, soy milk, tempeh) is associated with cardiovascular benefit including modest blood pressure reduction. For hypertensive patients, soy-based proteins are an excellent choice compared to salt-heavy processed foods. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Extremely low GI (~15) when boiled — among the lowest of any food. Very high protein (36.5g/100g dry) and healthy fat (19.9g), which together dramatically slow carbohydrate absorption. Soy isoflavones may also improve insulin sensitivity. Soybeans and soy products are recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes. Plain firm tofu, unsweetened soy milk, and tempeh are excellent daily protein sources. Fermented soy products (tempeh, miso in small amounts) provide additional health benefits. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Exceptional mineral content: highest magnesium (280mg), calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), phosphorus (704mg), zinc (4.9mg) of any common legume. Soy isoflavones have weak anti-inflammatory properties. Soy products are recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a complete plant protein source. Tofu and tempeh are the most accessible forms in Singapore. Note: some patients with thyroid conditions should moderate soy intake due to possible interference with thyroid hormone absorption when consumed in very large amounts. Note: Values per 100g dry raw soybeans. CRITICAL: Raw soybeans contain trypsin inhibitors and haemagglutinins that must be deactivated by thorough cooking (boiling 3–4 hours or pressure cooking 45–60 minutes). Soybeans are one of the 14 major allergens regulated in Singapore. Contains complete protein (all essential amino acids) — the only plant food to do so at comparable quality to animal proteins.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chicken Breast (raw, skinless, boneless)**. Raw skinless boneless chicken breast is the leanest animal protein commonly available in Singapore. With 23.1g protein and only 1.2g fat per 100g, it has one of the best protein-to-calorie ratios of any meat. Zero carbohydrate content. The white pectoralis major and minor muscles from the breast of a broiler chicken. Extremely versatile — used in virtually every cuisine style available in Singapore. Key nutritional features: high niacin (12.3mg/100g — essential for NAD+ metabolism), high vitamin B6 (0.87mg), and useful selenium (26.9mcg). The primary reference lean protein for clinical dietetic calculations in Singapore, used as the basis for protein intake targets in hospital meal planning. Per 100g: 110.0 kcal, 23.1g protein, 1.2g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 74.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Raw chicken breast is low in sodium (74mg/100g) and saturated fat. It is a lean white meat protein that does not contribute to blood pressure. The main sodium risk comes from marinades, sauces, and cooking preparations. Chicken breast is an excellent protein choice for patients with hypertension. Prepare by steaming, boiling, grilling, or baking without high-sodium marinades. Hainanese chicken rice (poached) is a good hawker preparation for hypertensive patients when consumed with minimal sauce. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Contains zero carbohydrate and has no effect on blood glucose. Very high in protein (23.1g/100g raw), which promotes satiety and supports muscle mass maintenance — crucial for preventing insulin resistance. Chicken breast is an ideal protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Regular intake of lean poultry protein supports metabolic health, prevents muscle wasting, and promotes satiety. Avoid fried chicken preparations which add significant carbohydrates (batter) and inflammatory oils. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Good source of selenium (26.9mcg/100g), vitamin B6 (0.87mg), and vitamin B12 (0.3mcg). Selenium is an antioxidant co-factor. Low in pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid (very low total fat 1.2g). Lean chicken breast is recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a primary protein source. Lower in pro-inflammatory saturated fat than red meat. Selenium content supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant activity. Prefer steamed or poached chicken preparations over deep-fried. Note: Values per 100g raw skinless boneless chicken breast. Cooking causes approximately 25–30% weight loss (moisture loss). Cooked chicken breast has approximately 165 kcal/100g. Cholesterol content (73mg/100g) is within acceptable limits for most patients. Halal-certified chicken is widely available in Singapore and is the standard for most hawker preparations.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chicken Thigh (raw, skinless, boneless)**. Raw skinless boneless chicken thigh is dark poultry meat from the upper leg of the chicken. It contains approximately 4.5x more fat than chicken breast (5.5g vs 1.2g per 100g), resulting in a richer flavour, juicier texture, and greater moisture retention during cooking. Preferred over breast in many Southeast Asian preparations — nasi lemak ayam, chicken biryani, satay, sambal ayam, and rendang — because it does not dry out during long cooking. Higher in zinc, riboflavin, and iron than breast. Still contains zero carbohydrates. The fat is a mix of saturated (1.5g), monounsaturated (1.9g), and polyunsaturated (1.2g), making it a balanced fat profile at reasonable serving sizes. Per 100g: 130.0 kcal, 18.8g protein, 5.5g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 93.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Chicken thigh is low to moderate in sodium (93mg/100g raw) and saturated fat (1.5g/100g). Higher in fat than breast but still a lean protein choice overall. The fat profile includes beneficial monounsaturated fat (1.9g) comparable to olive oil. Chicken thigh is an acceptable protein for patients with hypertension. Slightly higher in saturated fat than breast (1.5g vs 0.3g per 100g) but within reasonable limits. Skin-on preparations significantly increase saturated fat and calories and should be limited. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Contains zero carbohydrate. Higher fat content than breast slightly reduces the insulin response to the meal, promoting longer satiety. Good protein source for muscle maintenance. Chicken thigh is a good choice for patients with type 2 diabetes who find breast meat too dry — the higher fat content (5.5g/100g) improves palatability and satiety without adding carbohydrates. Remove skin before eating to reduce saturated fat. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Slightly higher in zinc (1.9mg vs 0.8mg breast) and riboflavin (0.20mg vs 0.10mg). Dark meat (thigh) contains more haem iron (0.8mg vs 0.5mg breast) and myoglobin. The polyunsaturated fat (1.2g/100g) includes some omega-6 arachidonic acid, which is pro-inflammatory in excess. Chicken thigh is acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate amounts. The slightly higher arachidonic acid content compared to breast is not significant at normal serving sizes. Higher flavour intensity makes it more palatable and supports dietary adherence. Note: Values per 100g raw skinless boneless chicken thigh. Adding skin approximately doubles the fat content (to ~10–12g/100g) and increases saturated fat substantially. Chicken thigh is slightly more economical than breast in Singapore. Cooking loss is approximately 25–30%.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Beef Topside (raw, lean, trimmed of visible fat)**. Beef topside is a lean cut from the inner thigh (round) of cattle, trimmed of visible fat. A standard reference lean beef cut used by both HPB Singapore and USDA for nutritional comparison purposes. With 20.7g protein and only 4.0g fat per 100g raw, it is one of the leaner beef cuts available. Exceptional zinc content (4.5mg/100g) — the best non-shellfish zinc source commonly available. The primary haem iron source in beef at 2.1mg/100g, which is much more bioavailable (15–35% absorption) than plant-based non-haem iron. Key source of vitamin B12 (2.4mcg/100g) — essential for patients on plant-based diets to supplement. Used in Singapore in beef pho, beef rendang (which traditionally uses chuck), beef steak, and stir-fried beef with vegetables. Per 100g: 127.0 kcal, 20.7g protein, 4.0g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 56.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Lean beef topside is relatively low in sodium (56mg/100g raw) and saturated fat (1.6g/100g). The haem iron content (2.1mg/100g) and zinc (4.5mg/100g) are nutritionally valuable. The main cardiovascular concern is the saturated fat content which is higher than chicken breast but lower than pork belly. Lean beef can be included in the diets of hypertensive patients in moderate quantities (2–3 servings per week). Prepare by grilling, steaming, or stir-frying without added salt or salty sauces. Avoid red meat daily — alternate with poultry and fish for cardiovascular benefit. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Zero carbohydrate. Lean beef is an important source of haem iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 for patients with type 2 diabetes who may have concurrent micronutrient deficiencies. Regular red meat consumption (>3 servings/week) is weakly associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk in large epidemiological studies. Lean beef is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes in moderate quantities (2–3 servings/week). Unprocessed lean beef is significantly preferable to processed beef products (sausages, burgers, canned beef) which have higher sodium and saturated fat. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Very high in zinc (4.5mg/100g) — zinc supports immune function and wound healing, important for RA patients. Very high in vitamin B12 (2.4mcg/100g). However, lean red meat consumption is associated with modest increases in inflammatory markers. Lean beef can be consumed 2–3 times per week by patients with rheumatoid arthritis for its zinc and vitamin B12 content. More than 3 servings per week of red meat has been associated with increased disease activity. For anti-inflammatory benefit, replace at least 2 weekly servings of red meat with oily fish. Note: Values per 100g raw lean beef topside. Halal beef is widely available in Singapore. The haem iron in beef has 3–5x higher bioavailability than non-haem iron from plants. Zinc is also highly bioavailable from beef. Very high vitamin B12 (2.4mcg/100g) — an important consideration for patients transitioning to semi-vegetarian diets.

Avoid red meat daily — alternate with poultry and fish for cardiovascular benefit. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Zero carbohydrate. Lean beef is an important source of haem iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 for patients with type 2 diabetes who may have concurrent micronutrient deficiencies. Regular red meat consumption (>3 servings/week) is weakly associated with increased type 2 diabetes risk in large epidemiological studies. Lean beef is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes in moderate quantities (2–3 servings/week). Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Very high in zinc (4.5mg/100g) — zinc supports immune function and wound healing, important for RA patients. Very high in vitamin B12 (2.4mcg/100g). However, lean red meat consumption is associated with modest increases in inflammatory markers. Lean beef can be consumed 2–3 times per week by patients with rheumatoid arthritis for its zinc and vitamin B12 content. More than 3 servings per week of red meat has been associated with increased disease activity. For anti-inflammatory benefit, replace at least 2 weekly servings of red meat with oily fish. Note: Values per 100g raw lean beef topside. Halal beef is widely available in Singapore. The haem iron in beef has 3–5x higher bioavailability than non-haem iron from plants. image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Mutton / Lamb (raw, bone-in, average mixed cut)**. Mutton refers to meat from adult sheep (over 1 year) in Singapore and South Asian contexts, though the term is sometimes applied loosely to goat meat. Distinguished from lamb (meat from animals under 1 year). Bone-in mixed cut values represent an average across leg, shoulder, and rib/rack cuts — the most commonly sold configuration at Singapore halal butchers and Indian wet market stalls. Higher in saturated fat than chicken but lower than pork belly. Notable zinc (4.2mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (2.2mcg/100g) content. Used in mutton biryani, mutton curry, sambal kambing, and the festive Eid al-Adha preparations. Halal-certified. The trans fat content (0.3g/100g) is from naturally occurring vaccenic acid in ruminant fat — considered less harmful than industrial trans fats. Per 100g: 145.0 kcal, 17.9g protein, 8.1g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 72.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Mutton has moderate saturated fat (3.7g/100g bone-in raw — higher on boneless basis). Regular heavy consumption of red meat including mutton is associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes. Preparations such as mutton biryani and mutton curry typically add significant sodium from spice blends and salt. Patients with hypertension should limit mutton to 1–2 servings per week and select lean pieces over fatty cuts. Avoid heavy coconut milk-based mutton preparations which add significant saturated fat. Prefer dry-spiced preparations or watery curry bases over rich korma-style dishes. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Zero carbohydrate. Good source of protein (17.9g/100g bone-in raw) and zinc (4.2mg). Red meat consumption (including mutton) at moderate levels (2–3 servings/week) is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes. Mutton is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes in moderate amounts. The protein content supports muscle maintenance and glycaemic control. Select lean cuts and trim visible fat. Avoid processed mutton products (sausages, preserved mutton) which add sodium. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Very high in zinc (4.2mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (2.2mcg/100g). Contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 precursor) which may contribute to inflammation at very high intakes. Red meat consumption is associated with modestly increased inflammatory markers. Mutton is acceptable 1–2 times per week for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Its zinc and B12 content are beneficial. For patients with active joint inflammation, reducing red meat to once per week and replacing with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit. Note: Values per 100g raw bone-in mutton. Bone content in bone-in cuts is typically 20–35% by weight, meaning the edible meat per 100g bone-in is approximately 65–80g. Trans fat (0.3g/100g) is naturally occurring vaccenic acid from ruminant fat — not industrially produced — and is considered less harmful. Halal-certified. Not kosher (pork products are kosher-prohibited but beef and lamb require specific slaughter method).

Patients with hypertension should limit mutton to 1–2 servings per week and select lean pieces over fatty cuts. Avoid heavy coconut milk-based mutton preparations which add significant saturated fat. Prefer dry-spiced preparations or watery curry bases over rich korma-style dishes. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Zero carbohydrate. Good source of protein (17.9g/100g bone-in raw) and zinc (4.2mg). Red meat consumption (including mutton) at moderate levels (2–3 servings/week) is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes. Mutton is acceptable for patients with type 2 diabetes in moderate amounts. The protein content supports muscle maintenance and glycaemic control. Select lean cuts and trim visible fat. Avoid processed mutton products (sausages, preserved mutton) which add sodium. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Very high in zinc (4.2mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (2.2mcg/100g). Contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 precursor) which may contribute to inflammation at very high intakes. Red meat consumption is associated with modestly increased inflammatory markers. Mutton is acceptable 1–2 times per week for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Its zinc and B12 content are beneficial. For patients with active joint inflammation, reducing red meat to once per week and replacing with oily fish (salmon, mackerel) provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit. Note: Values per 100g raw bone-in mutton. Bone content in bone-in cuts is typically 20–35% by weight, meaning the edible meat per 100g bone-in is approximately 65–80g. Trans fat (0.3g/100g) is naturally occurring vaccenic acid from ruminant fat — not industrially produced — and is considered less harmful. Halal-certified. Not kosher (pork products are kosher-prohibited but beef and lamb require specific slaughter method).

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Chicken Egg (whole, raw, size 60g)**. The whole chicken egg (Gallus gallus domesticus) is arguably the most complete single food available — providing all essential amino acids in nearly perfect proportions (biological value ~100), along with 13 essential vitamins and minerals. The edible portion (white + yolk, no shell) of a 60g size-2 egg weighs approximately 50g. Raw egg white contains avidin, which blocks biotin absorption — cooking destroys avidin and makes all egg nutrients fully available. Very high in selenium (31.7mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.46mg), vitamin D (2.0mcg), and vitamin B12 (1.1mcg). High cholesterol (372mg/100g) is in the yolk — but dietary cholesterol has a much smaller effect on blood cholesterol than once believed. Used in virtually every Singapore dish category. Per 100g: 143.0 kcal, 12.6g protein, 9.5g fat, 0.7g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 142.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. The high cholesterol content of eggs (372mg/100g — an egg has ~186mg cholesterol) was previously a major concern. Current clinical guidelines (2020+) have substantially revised the egg-cholesterol-cardiovascular relationship, noting that dietary cholesterol has a smaller effect on blood cholesterol than saturated fat. Eggs are relatively low in saturated fat (3.1g/100g). The sodium content is moderate (142mg/100g edible). Current Singapore HPB guidelines suggest up to 1 egg daily is acceptable for most healthy adults including those with hypertension, with minimal cardiovascular risk. Patients with established dyslipidaemia or diabetes may benefit from monitoring egg intake (1 egg/day or 5–7 eggs/week maximum). The overall diet pattern (saturated fat, sodium, fibre) is more important than egg quantity alone. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Essentially no carbohydrate (0.7g/100g). High protein (12.6g/100g) and moderate fat that promote satiety. Current clinical evidence does not support limiting eggs for patients with type 2 diabetes at up to 1 egg per day. Eggs are among the most nutrient-dense low-carbohydrate foods. Eggs are an excellent breakfast protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Replacing high-carbohydrate breakfasts (kaya toast + white bread, nasi lemak) with 2 eggs + vegetables dramatically improves morning blood glucose management. Prepare by boiling, poaching, or scrambling — avoid frying in excessive oil. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Eggs are the richest food source of biotin and the most bioavailable source of choline. High selenium (31.7mcg/100g), riboflavin (0.46mg), vitamin D (2.0mcg), and vitamin B12 (1.1mcg). Vitamin D is particularly relevant for RA patients who are commonly deficient. Eggs are strongly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a nutrient-dense whole food. The vitamin D content (2.0mcg/100g), while modest, contributes to the 15–20mcg/day target important for RA patients. Choline in egg yolk supports cell membrane integrity. The pro-inflammatory concern of egg arachidonic acid is not clinically significant at normal intake levels. Note: Values per 100g edible portion (white + yolk, shell excluded). A standard size-2 egg (60g total with shell) has approximately 50g edible portion. Raw egg white contains avidin which blocks biotin absorption — cooking is recommended. Salmonella risk from raw eggs is low in Singapore (pasteurised eggs available) but cooking is advised for high-risk individuals (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant). Egg is a major food allergen.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Firm Tofu (fresh, plain, regular, pressed)**. Firm tofu (tau kwa in Hokkien) is fresh tofu made by coagulating hot soy milk with a coagulant (calcium sulfate or nigari/magnesium chloride), then pressing the resulting curds into a firm, dense block. Higher in calcium when coagulated with calcium sulfate (the most common commercial method) — values in this record assume calcium-set tofu. With 8.1g protein and 4.2g fat per 100g, it is a nutritionally excellent plant protein with essentially zero carbohydrate. Exceptional calcium content (350mg/100g) — comparable to cow's milk. Extremely versatile: used raw in Japanese cold tofu (hiyayakko), deep-fried in tofu pok (fried tofu puffs), grilled, stir-fried, braised, in soups, and as a meat substitute in curries and stews. Per 100g: 76.0 kcal, 8.1g protein, 4.2g fat, 1.9g carbohydrates, 0.3g dietary fibre, 7.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 15 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 0.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of firm tofu (~15). The negligible carbohydrate content (1.9g/100g) means the glycaemic load per serving is essentially zero regardless of GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low sodium (7mg/100g). Excellent calcium source (350mg/100g — comparable to milk). Soy proteins and isoflavones may have modest blood pressure-lowering effects through endothelial function improvement. Firm tofu is an excellent food for patients with hypertension. Very low in sodium, high in calcium, and a complete protein source. When prepared as agedashi tofu (fried) or in salty sauces, the sodium from cooking preparations overrides the tofu's inherent low sodium. Prefer steamed, boiled, or braised tofu preparations with minimal added salt. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Essentially no carbohydrate (1.9g/100g), very low GI (~15). High protein (8.1g/100g) for a plant food. Soy isoflavones may improve insulin sensitivity. An excellent protein substitute for high-carbohydrate dishes. Firm tofu is highly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes as a daily protein source. It can be used as a protein replacement for rice in mixed dishes (tofu with vegetables + 50% less rice dramatically reduces meal glycaemic load). Silken tofu is suitable for smoothies and desserts as a protein addition. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Excellent calcium source (350mg/100g) — important for bone health in RA patients at risk of osteoporosis. Soy isoflavones have weak anti-inflammatory properties. image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Tempeh (fresh, plain, uncooked)**. Tempeh is a traditional fermented soybean product originating from Java, Indonesia. Whole soybeans are cooked, dehulled, inoculated with Rhizopus oligosporus mould spores, and incubated for 24–48 hours. The mould binds the soybeans into a compact, firm white-grey cake with a distinctly nutty, earthy, slightly mushroom-like aroma. The fermentation process substantially improves nutritional quality compared to raw soybeans: reduces phytate content (improving mineral absorption), increases riboflavin, niacin, and B6, and enhances protein digestibility. With 19g protein and 10.8g fat per 100g, it is the highest-protein plant food commonly available in Singapore markets after raw soybeans. Sold fresh (refrigerated) in Singapore at wet markets and Cold Storage, usually in 200–250g slabs. The standard preparation in Singapore and Malaysia is tempeh goreng (deep-fried slices, often with sambal or sweet sauce). Per 100g: 193.0 kcal, 19.0g protein, 10.8g fat, 9.4g carbohydrates, 1.4g dietary fibre, 9.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 15 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 1.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of tempeh (~15). Very low carbohydrate content and the intact soybean cell matrix result in extremely low glycaemic impact. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low sodium (9mg/100g raw). The fermentation process increases bioavailability of all nutrients. Soy isoflavones in tempeh have demonstrated modest antihypertensive effects in clinical studies. Plain tempeh is excellent for patients with hypertension. The sodium concern arises from preparation — tempeh goreng (fried with sambal/soy sauce) adds significant sodium. Prefer steamed, baked, or lightly stir-fried preparations with minimal added salt to retain its low-sodium benefit. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low GI (~15). High protein (19g/100g). Fermentation partially breaks down the oligosaccharides and phytates, improving mineral absorption and digestibility. The low carbohydrate content (9.4g/100g) and high fat and protein make it an excellent diabetic-friendly protein food. Tempeh is highly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes. It provides more protein than tofu (19g vs 8g per 100g), very low GI, and good dietary fat profile. Daily tempeh consumption as a protein source is one of the most effective Indonesian/Malaysian dietary strategies for blood glucose management. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Fermentation increases riboflavin (0.36mg/100g) and niacin (2.6mg/100g). High magnesium (81mg/100g) and calcium (111mg/100g). Soy isoflavones have weak anti-inflammatory properties. Higher vitamin E than raw soybeans (2.2mg/100g). Tempeh is recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as a fermented, high-protein plant food. The fermentation process may enhance the anti-inflammatory profile compared to unfermented soy products. Good magnesium content supports muscle and nerve function relevant to RA management. Note: Values per 100g fresh uncooked tempeh. The fermentation significantly changes the nutritional profile compared to raw soybeans — particularly reducing phytates and increasing B vitamin content. Deep-frying (tempeh goreng) increases fat content substantially. Some tempeh is made with mixed grains (rice + soy) — this record is for pure soybean tempeh.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Salmon Fillet (raw, Atlantic, farmed)**. Atlantic farmed salmon (Salmo salar) is the most widely consumed oily fish in Singapore, sold fresh at supermarkets and wet markets, as sashimi at Japanese restaurants, and as poke bowl protein. The pink-orange flesh colour comes from carotenoid pigments (astaxanthin). Farmed salmon has a higher fat content (13.4g/100g) than wild Atlantic salmon (~8g/100g), providing more absolute omega-3 fatty acids per serving despite a slightly lower omega-3 percentage. Exceptional nutrient profile: 20.4g protein, 11.1mcg vitamin D (one of the richest food sources), 4.0mcg vitamin B12, 41.4mcg selenium, and 3.6mg vitamin E per 100g raw. The omega-3 EPA+DHA content (~2.2g per 150g serving) meets the therapeutic anti-inflammatory dose recommended by major health organisations. Per 100g: 206.0 kcal, 20.4g protein, 13.4g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 59.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Salmon is the richest food source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid). These fatty acids reduce triglycerides, modestly lower blood pressure, and reduce platelet aggregation — all cardiovascular benefits. Very high in potassium (490mg/100g). Salmon is strongly recommended for patients with hypertension as part of a heart-healthy diet. Two 150g servings per week of oily fish (salmon, mackerel, tuna) is the clinically endorsed target from HPB Singapore and all major cardiovascular guidelines. Low sodium (59mg/100g raw) — avoid high-sodium salmon preparations (smoked salmon, salt-cured salmon) which can reverse the cardiovascular benefit. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Zero carbohydrate. Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA) may improve insulin sensitivity. Very high protein (20.4g/100g) and healthy fats promote satiety. Excellent selenium (41.4mcg/100g) which supports antioxidant defences. Salmon is highly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes. Replacing one weekly red meat meal with salmon provides substantial omega-3 benefit. The vitamin B12 (4.0mcg/100g) and vitamin D (11.1mcg/100g) content addresses common micronutrient deficiencies in type 2 diabetes. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Atlantic farmed salmon has approximately 2.2–2.5g EPA+DHA per 150g serving — the highest omega-3 dose of any commonly consumed fish. EPA and DHA are clinically validated anti-inflammatory fatty acids that reduce joint inflammation and morning stiffness in rheumatoid arthritis patients. High vitamin D (11.1mcg/100g) and selenium (41.4mcg/100g). Salmon is the highest-priority fish for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. At least 2 servings per week (150g each) is the clinical minimum for anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit. Patients who cannot consume sufficient oily fish should consider omega-3 fish oil supplementation (EPA+DHA 1–3g/day). Prefer grilled, steamed, or poached salmon over smoked preparations. Note: Values per 100g raw Atlantic farmed salmon fillet (skinless). Farmed salmon has slightly higher fat and omega-3 content than wild-caught due to controlled diet. The total polyunsaturated fat (4.2g/100g) includes approximately 1.5g EPA and 1.0g DHA per 100g — approximately 2.2–2.5g EPA+DHA per 150g serving. The vitamin D content (11.1mcg/100g) makes salmon one of the best dietary vitamin D sources available.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Indian Mackerel / Kembong (raw, edible flesh)**. Indian mackerel (kembong/kembung, Rastrelliger kanagurta) is a small, torpedo-shaped oily fish endemic to the tropical waters of the Indian Ocean and Western Pacific. Widely caught in Singapore waters and sold fresh daily at wet markets — one of the most affordable oily fish available in Singapore. The flesh is dark and flavourful with a moderate fish oil content. High in vitamin B12 (7.6mcg/100g — among the highest of commonly consumed fish) and selenium (36.5mcg/100g). Omega-3 content lower than salmon per 100g but significantly higher than white fish. Commonly prepared in Singapore as fried kembong (goreng), kembong in sambal, fish curry, and in traditional Malay preparations. Commonly consumed whole with the soft bones when pressure-cooked, providing calcium. Per 100g: 115.0 kcal, 20.0g protein, 3.5g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 72.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Indian mackerel is a small oily fish rich in omega-3 EPA and DHA which reduce cardiovascular risk. Low in sodium (72mg/100g raw). Affordable and widely available in Singapore wet markets — making it a practical omega-3 source for lower-income populations. Kembong is strongly recommended for patients with hypertension. Two servings per week provides meaningful cardiovascular benefit. The salt added during frying or curry preparation should be minimised. Kembong sambal and asam fish preparations can have high sodium — request reduced salt. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Zero carbohydrate. Omega-3 fatty acids support insulin sensitivity. Very high vitamin B12 (7.6mcg/100g) and selenium (36.5mcg/100g) address common micronutrient deficiencies in type 2 diabetes. Indian mackerel is highly recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes as a weekly protein source. More affordable than salmon while providing meaningful omega-3 benefit. Preferred preparation is steaming or grilling with minimal salt. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Good omega-3 EPA+DHA content for a small fish. Exceptionally high vitamin B12 (7.6mcg/100g) and selenium (36.5mcg/100g). Vitamin D (4.5mcg/100g) supports immune regulation and bone health. Indian mackerel is an affordable anti-inflammatory protein source for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. While lower in absolute omega-3 than salmon per serving, regular consumption (2–3 times per week) contributes meaningfully to the anti-inflammatory diet pattern. The high vitamin D and B12 content provides additional benefit. Note: Values per 100g raw edible flesh (head, spine, larger bones removed). Small pin bones in mackerel are often consumed when pressure-cooked or when fried crispy, adding calcium. Very high vitamin B12 (7.6mcg/100g) — a 90g serving provides 68mcg B12, far exceeding the 2.4mcg adult daily requirement.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Red Snapper / Ang Ko Li (raw fillet)**. Red snapper (ang ko li in Hokkien, ikan merah in Malay) is a popular mild-flavoured white fish sold whole or as fillets at Singapore wet markets and supermarkets. The firm white flesh is low in fat (1.3g/100g), very high in protein (20.5g/100g), and has a clean, delicate flavour that makes it ideal for steaming, poaching, and gentle preparations. Used in Cantonese steamed whole fish, fish head curry, Teochew steamed fish, and fish soup (sliced fillet). Very high in selenium (38.2mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (3.8mcg/100g), and a good source of potassium (444mg/100g). Red snapper from Singapore waters is typically smaller than the Gulf snapper (Lutjanus campechanus) used in Western cuisines. Per 100g: 100.0 kcal, 20.5g protein, 1.3g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 64.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very lean white fish (1.3g fat/100g). Very low sodium (64mg/100g raw) and very high potassium (444mg/100g). Excellent lean protein for cardiovascular health. Red snapper is an excellent protein choice for patients with hypertension. Steamed red snapper with ginger and spring onion (Cantonese style) adds minimal sodium from the light soy drizzle. Fish head curry preparations add significant sodium — request reduced sauce. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Zero carbohydrate. High lean protein (20.5g/100g). Very low fat. Selenium (38.2mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (3.8mcg/100g) are valuable for metabolic health. Red snapper is an excellent lean protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Steamed fish preparations are preferable to deep-fried versions which add significant fat and carbohydrate from batter. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. While red snapper is not an oily fish (lower omega-3 than salmon or mackerel), it provides useful selenium (38.2mcg/100g) and vitamin E (1.7mg/100g). These antioxidants support anti-inflammatory defences. Red snapper is a good lean white fish option for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. It complements oily fish in the weekly protein rotation — use oily fish (salmon, mackerel) as the primary omega-3 source and white fish (snapper, sea bass) as additional lean protein. Note: Values per 100g raw fillet, skinless. Red snapper is a catch-all name in Singapore — multiple Lutjanus species are sold as 'ang ko li' or 'ikan merah'. Nutritional values are broadly consistent across species. Low in omega-3 compared to oily fish like salmon or mackerel. Very low in mercury — safe for consumption multiple times per week including during pregnancy.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Black Tiger Prawn (raw, shell-off, deveined)**. Black tiger prawns (udang harimau, Penaeus monodon) are the largest and most commonly sold prawn variety in Singapore wet markets. Identifiable by their dark striped body (brown-black banding). Shell-off, deveined prawns are available fresh or frozen at Singapore supermarkets. Very lean protein (17.8g/100g) with minimal fat (0.9g/100g). Higher cholesterol than fin fish (152mg/100g) but dietary cholesterol from seafood has limited effect on blood cholesterol. High selenium (38mcg/100g) and moderate vitamin A (54mcg RAE/100g). Used in Singapore prawn noodles (Hokkien hae mee), sambal prawns, Singapore chilli prawns, pad thai, and various stir-fries. The shells and heads (when sautéed) make intensely flavourful prawn stock. Per 100g: 82.0 kcal, 17.8g protein, 0.9g fat, 0.9g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 119.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Raw prawns are relatively low in sodium (119mg/100g shell-off). The cholesterol content (152mg/100g) was historically a concern but dietary cholesterol from shellfish has minimal effect on blood cholesterol for most people. The main hypertension concern is the preparation — sambal prawns, har cheong gai (prawn paste chicken), and prawn paste preparations add very high sodium. Plain raw prawns are acceptable for patients with hypertension. Avoid prawn paste (hae ko), sambal, and heavily salted preparations. Steamed or poached prawns with minimal sauce are the best preparation for hypertensive patients. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low calorie (82 kcal/100g), very lean protein (17.8g/100g), minimal carbohydrate (0.9g/100g). Excellent for body weight management critical in type 2 diabetes. High selenium (38.0mcg/100g). Prawns are an excellent low-calorie, low-carbohydrate protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Very high protein-to-calorie ratio promotes satiety. Avoid deep-fried prawn preparations (har gao, prawn fritters) which add significant refined carbohydrate and fat. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. High selenium (38.0mcg/100g) and vitamin E (2.0mg/100g) support antioxidant defences. Contains omega-3 fatty acids (predominantly as phospholipids). High vitamin A (54mcg RAE/100g). Prawns are acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis at moderate intake levels. They contribute to a varied seafood rotation alongside oily fish. The antioxidant selenium content is particularly valuable. Note: Values per 100g raw shell-off deveined black tiger prawn flesh. Crustaceans are a major allergen in Singapore. Not kosher (shellfish are not kosher). The high cholesterol (152mg/100g) is now considered less clinically significant than once thought — dietary cholesterol from seafood has minimal impact on blood LDL for most individuals. Deveining removes the digestive tract (black line) — this does not significantly affect nutritional values.

Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Black Tiger Prawn (raw, shell-off, deveined)**. Black tiger prawns (udang harimau, Penaeus monodon) are the largest and most commonly sold prawn variety in Singapore wet markets. Identifiable by their dark striped body (brown-black banding). Shell-off, deveined prawns are available fresh or frozen at Singapore supermarkets. Very lean protein (17.8g/100g) with minimal fat (0.9g/100g). Higher cholesterol than fin fish (152mg/100g) but dietary cholesterol from seafood has limited effect on blood cholesterol. High selenium (38mcg/100g) and moderate vitamin A (54mcg RAE/100g). Used in Singapore prawn noodles (Hokkien hae mee), sambal prawns, Singapore chilli prawns, pad thai, and various stir-fries. The shells and heads (when sautéed) make intensely flavourful prawn stock. Per 100g: 82.0 kcal, 17.8g protein, 0.9g fat, 0.9g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 119.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Raw prawns are relatively low in sodium (119mg/100g shell-off). The cholesterol content (152mg/100g) was historically a concern but dietary cholesterol from shellfish has minimal effect on blood cholesterol for most people. The main hypertension concern is the preparation — sambal prawns, har cheong gai (prawn paste chicken), and prawn paste preparations add very high sodium. Plain raw prawns are acceptable for patients with hypertension. Avoid prawn paste (hae ko), sambal, and heavily salted preparations. Steamed or poached prawns with minimal sauce are the best preparation for hypertensive patients. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Very low calorie (82 kcal/100g), very lean protein (17.8g/100g), minimal carbohydrate (0.9g/100g). Excellent for body weight management critical in type 2 diabetes. High selenium (38.0mcg/100g). Prawns are an excellent low-calorie, low-carbohydrate protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Very high protein-to-calorie ratio promotes satiety. Avoid deep-fried prawn preparations (har gao, prawn fritters) which add significant refined carbohydrate and fat. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. High selenium (38.0mcg/100g) and vitamin E (2.0mg/100g) support antioxidant defences. Contains omega-3 fatty acids (predominantly as phospholipids). High vitamin A (54mcg RAE/100g). Prawns are acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis at moderate intake levels. They contribute to a varied seafood rotation alongside oily fish. The antioxidant selenium content is particularly valuable. Note: Values per 100g raw shell-off deveined black tiger prawn flesh. Crustaceans are a major allergen in Singapore. Not kosher (shellfish are not kosher). The high cholesterol (152mg/100g) is now considered less clinically significant than once thought — dietary cholesterol from seafood has minimal impact on blood LDL for most individuals. Deveining removes the digestive tract (black line) — this does not significantly affect nutritional values.

image_purpose: Primary identification and classification.

content.text: I have taken a photo of my meal (Hero Shot (Raw/Whole)). Please identify this food and provide: (1) its name and a brief description, (2) nutritional profile per 100g and per serving, (3) glycemic index and glycemic load with source, (4) health guidance for patients with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.

This is **Yellowfin Tuna (raw, sashimi grade fillet)**. Yellowfin tuna (Thunnus albacares, ahi tuna) is a large predatory fish with deep red, lean flesh. Sashimi-grade yellowfin tuna is one of the most nutrient-dense proteins available: 23.4g protein, 1.0g fat, 0 carbohydrate per 100g raw. The selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) is extraordinary — among the highest of any food in the standard diet. Very high niacin (18.5mg/100g — exceeds the entire adult daily niacin requirement in a single 100g serving). Very high vitamin B6 (0.90mg) and magnesium (64mg/100g). Consumed in Singapore as sashimi, poke bowls, tuna nigiri, and canned tuna (in water or oil). The mercury content of large yellowfin tuna is moderate — limiting consumption to 2 servings per week is the standard public health recommendation. Per 100g: 109.0 kcal, 23.4g protein, 1.0g fat, 0.0g carbohydrates, 0.0g dietary fibre, 45.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Beneficial. Very lean white-fish-like protein (1.0g fat/100g) with high potassium (444mg/100g) and very low sodium (45mg/100g). Omega-3 EPA+DHA content at levels relevant to cardiovascular benefit. Exceptionally high niacin (18.5mg/100g) supports vascular health. Yellowfin tuna is excellent for patients with hypertension. The omega-3 content supports cardiovascular benefit. However, tuna should be limited to 2 servings per week for some groups (pregnant women, young children) due to moderate mercury content — mercury exposure is a consideration for large predatory fish like tuna. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Beneficial. Zero carbohydrate. Extraordinary selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) — among the highest of any commonly consumed food. High niacin (18.5mg/100g) and vitamin B6 (0.90mg/100g). Selenium and niacin support insulin signalling pathways. Tuna is highly recommended as a lean protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Canned tuna in water (drained) is a convenient, affordable alternative to fresh sashimi with nearly equivalent nutritional value. Limit to 2 servings per week due to mercury. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Beneficial. Extraordinary selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) — one of the highest selenium foods available. A single 80g sashimi serving provides 87mcg selenium against a 55–70mcg daily target. Selenium is a critical co-factor for anti-inflammatory antioxidant enzymes. Omega-3 EPA+DHA provide anti-inflammatory benefit. Tuna is particularly valuable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its exceptional selenium content supporting glutathione peroxidase activity. Omega-3 fatty acids provide direct anti-inflammatory joint benefit. Limit to 2 servings per week due to mercury — supplement with salmon, mackerel, and sardines for additional weekly seafood. Note: Values per 100g raw sashimi-grade yellowfin tuna fillet. The extraordinarily high selenium (108.2mcg/100g) reflects the bio-concentration of dietary selenium by tuna throughout the food chain. A 100g serving provides almost 2x the adult daily selenium requirement (55mcg). Mercury note: large yellowfin tuna are at the top of the food chain — mercury bio-concentrates. HPB Singapore recommends limiting large predatory fish (tuna, shark, swordfish) to 2 servings per week for most adults, and limiting further for pregnant women and young children.
